description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Hiding from a custom list is possible on who sees our post is possible making victim not remove them from the list.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can create custom friend lists for post privacy and then deactivate their account.
prerequisites: Attacker has an active Facebook account.
Victim has an active Facebook account.
Victim uses custom friend lists for post privacy.
applicability_signals: The option to select 'specific friends' for post visibility exists.
The ability to create and manage custom friend lists is present.
skip_if: The platform automatically removes inactive users from all friend lists upon account deactivation.

recon_steps: Identify the post privacy settings feature on Facebook.
Explore the functionality for creating and managing custom friend lists for post visibility.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a post with visibility set to a specific custom friend list. Ensure the attacker's account is included in this list.
Step 2: Deactivate the attacker's Facebook account.
Step 3: The victim attempts to update the custom friend list for a new post.
Step 4: Observe that the victim cannot remove the deactivated attacker's account from the list, as it no longer appears as an active user to be removed.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a behavioral exploit, not payload-based)
tools_recommended: Manual testing with two Facebook accounts (one attacker, one victim).
success_indicators: The victim cannot remove the attacker's account from the custom friend list when updating privacy settings for subsequent posts, even after the attacker's account is deactivated.
The attacker's deactivated account remains in the custom list, potentially preventing the victim from fully managing who sees their posts.
failure_indicators: If the victim's UI allows removal of deactivated accounts or automatically purges them from lists, the exploit fails. Pivot to other privacy-related logic flaws.
If the platform does not allow selection of inactive accounts in custom lists to begin with, this specific attack vector is not applicable.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can persist in a victim's custom friend list, preventing the victim from effectively managing who sees their posts. This could lead to unintended content exposure if the victim believes they have a controlled audience.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate the lifecycle management of user accounts within privacy control lists, specifically testing how deactivated or deleted accounts behave when interacting with these lists.

meta.title: Game On – Finding vulnerabilities in Valve’s “Steam Sockets”

tech_stack_fingerprints: Games using Steam Sockets (e.g., CS:GO, Dota2, Team Fortress 2, Destiny 2)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When interacting with online opponents or game servers that utilize Valve's Game Networking Sockets library for communication.
prerequisites: Attacker can establish network communication with the target game client or server.
applicability_signals: Target game supports online multiplayer functionality.
Game is known to use Steam Sockets.
skip_if: Target game does not have online multiplayer capabilities.
Target game uses a different networking library exclusively.

recon_steps: Identify games that are known to use Valve's Game Networking Sockets (GNS) library, either by official documentation, community discussions, or reverse engineering.
Determine if the target game uses GNS in peer-to-peer or client-server mode.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft and send specially malformed network packets to the target game client or server via the GNS library.
Step 2: Exploit the identified vulnerabilities (CVE-2020-6016 through CVE-2020-6019) within the GNS library's packet parsing or handling logic.
Step 3: For CVE-2020-6016, leverage specific C++ standard behaviors and GCC implementation details to achieve arbitrary code execution on a third-party game server.
payloads_or_patterns: Specially crafted malformed network packets targeting GNS vulnerabilities (specific payloads are not detailed in the writeup but would involve manipulating message structures and data fields based on the vulnerability).
tools_recommended: Custom network packet crafting tools
Network analysis tools (e.g., Wireshark)
Debugger for analyzing game client/server behavior
Reverse engineering tools (e.g., IDA Pro, Ghidra)
C++ compiler and understanding of C++ standard library and GCC internals
success_indicators: Remote crash of the opponent's game client (CVE-2020-6016, CVE-2020-6017).
Remote crash of the Valve game server (CVE-2020-6018).
Remote code execution on a third-party game server (CVE-2020-6016).
Takeover of all connected players' sessions after server compromise (CVE-2020-6016).
failure_indicators: No observable change in game client or server stability after sending malformed packets. → Investigate packet crafting and ensure correct GNS message structures are being targeted.
Game server or client gracefully handles malformed packets without crashing. → The specific vulnerability might not be present or is mitigated in the tested version.

playbooks.impact: Remote denial-of-service against game clients and servers, leading to forced wins or inability to play. Remote code execution on third-party game servers, allowing full control of the server and subsequently all connected players' sessions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with game networking libraries, pay close attention to packet parsing and handling logic, as subtle implementation details and language-specific features can lead to severe vulnerabilities like memory corruption and RCE.

meta.title: Facebook leak referrer data

affected_components: navigation via back button after clicking a link

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a link on Facebook contains a `back uri` parameter that is not properly handled by Facebook's linkshim and allows for redirection to an arbitrary URL.
prerequisites: Attacker must be logged into a Facebook account.
applicability_signals: Existence of a link with a `back uri` parameter.
Observing the source code of a link to identify if it bypasses linkshim.
skip_if: All links are correctly processed by Facebook's linkshim, preventing arbitrary URL redirection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to www.facebook.com and log in as UserOne.
Step 2: Identify a Facebook link that uses a `back uri` parameter.
Step 3: Inspect the source code of the link to see how the `back uri` parameter is handled.
Step 4: Observe if the `back uri` parameter bypasses Facebook's linkshim.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a URL that includes the target Facebook link with a `back uri` parameter pointing to an attacker-controlled URL.
Step 2: Click on the crafted Facebook link.
Step 3: Upon landing on the Facebook page, click the 'back' button in the top-left header.
Step 4: Observe if the redirection bypasses the linkshim and goes directly to the URL specified in the `back uri` parameter.
payloads_or_patterns: URL with `back uri` parameter bypassing linkshim: <TARGET_URL>/some/facebook/page?back_uri=<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_URL>
tools_recommended: Browser developer tools (for inspecting source code)
Burp Suite (for analyzing link parameters and potential redirections)
success_indicators: Successful redirection to the attacker-controlled URL after clicking the 'back' button, bypassing the Facebook linkshim interstitial page.
failure_indicators: The Facebook linkshim interstitial page is displayed, indicating the `back uri` parameter is correctly handled and not exploitable for open redirect.
The 'back' button redirects to an expected Facebook page instead of the `back uri` parameter's value.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can redirect Facebook users to arbitrary malicious websites by crafting special links that bypass Facebook's linkshim security mechanism when navigating back.
playbooks.key_lesson: When identifying redirect parameters, always verify if they bypass intermediary security measures like linkshims before assuming they are safe.

meta.title: Facebook push notification linkshim bypassed

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Facebook push notification link is clicked and the redirect URL is not properly validated, allowing for an open redirect.
prerequisites: An authenticated Facebook user account.
applicability_signals: Facebook push notification links present in the application.
Observation of a linkshim redirection mechanism.
skip_if: All external links are strictly validated and do not allow for redirection to arbitrary URLs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Obtain a Facebook push notification link that triggers the linkshim.
Step 2: Analyze the structure of the push notification link to identify the redirection parameter (e.g., 'endpoint').
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a URL by appending a malicious website to the 'endpoint' parameter of the Facebook push notification link.
Step 2: Attempt to bypass URL validation by using URL encoding on the malicious website.
Step 3: Further attempt to bypass validation by adding extra slashes ('%2f') to the URL before the malicious website.
Step 4: Send the crafted URL to the target user or trigger it via the push notification.
Step 5: Observe if the user is redirected to the attacker-controlled website.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, analyze URL parameters and observe redirection behavior.
Manual URL encoding tools: To experiment with different encoding schemes.
success_indicators: The user is successfully redirected to the attacker-controlled website instead of the intended Facebook page.
The response from the server confirms a successful redirection to the specified 'endpoint' parameter.
failure_indicators: The user is redirected to a Facebook error page or the original intended page, indicating the redirect was blocked.
A 403 Forbidden error is returned, suggesting a Web Application Firewall (WAF) or security mechanism is preventing the redirect.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can redirect Facebook users to malicious websites, leading to phishing attacks, credential harvesting, malware distribution, or other forms of social engineering.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all user-supplied URL parameters, especially those intended for redirection, to prevent open redirect vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Important, Spoofing - zero-click, wormable, cross-platform remote code execution in Microsoft Teams

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the attacker can send or edit a message that will be viewed by a victim using the Microsoft Teams desktop client.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Microsoft Teams account.
Victim has an authenticated Microsoft Teams account and views the attacker-controlled message.
applicability_signals: Vulnerability disclosed in Microsoft Teams, impacting the desktop client.
skip_if: Victim is not using the Microsoft Teams desktop client.
Microsoft Teams client has been patched.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze Microsoft Teams message structure and rendering for potential injection points, particularly in how custom content or external links are handled.
Step 2: Investigate how Teams processes messages containing specially crafted data, looking for parsing vulnerabilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious message containing a specially crafted payload designed to exploit a vulnerability in the Teams desktop client's message rendering or processing.
Step 2: Send the malicious message to a target user or group, or edit an existing message to contain the payload.
Step 3: The victim receives or views the message, triggering the exploit within their Teams desktop client.
Step 4: The crafted payload executes arbitrary code on the victim's machine with the privileges of the Teams application.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup. The writeup mentions a chain of bugs leading to RCE but does not detail the specific payload or exploit mechanism.
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup. Tools would likely involve custom scripting for message crafting and potentially network monitoring.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution on the victim's machine, confirmed by exfiltration of sensitive data, command output, or establishment of a reverse shell.
failure_indicators: Message is displayed normally without triggering any malicious behavior. The Teams client may display an error or sanitize the input.
If a 403 Forbidden or similar error is encountered when attempting to send a specially crafted message, it may indicate input sanitization or a WAF.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the victim's machine, allowing an attacker to take full control of the user's system, access sensitive data, and potentially spread laterally within the organization.
playbooks.key_lesson: Zero-click RCE vulnerabilities in widely used communication platforms like Microsoft Teams can have a significant impact and should be thoroughly investigated, even if initially rated low by the vendor.

meta.title: Stored XSS through navigation menu item edited in Customizer in Wordpress

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is a WordPress site and the attacker has access to edit the site's navigation menus, specifically the 'Social Media' links section.
prerequisites: Attacker has privileges to edit website content or customize menus.
applicability_signals: WordPress site is discoverable.
The 'Customize' option is available for the site.
The 'Menus' tab is present within the Customizer.
A 'Social Media' section with editable fields (like Facebook, Twitter, etc.) exists under Menus.
skip_if: Input fields in the 'Social Media' links section are properly sanitized and do not allow HTML or script execution.
The target WordPress version is patched against CVE-2019-17674 and CVE-2020-11025.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target WordPress site and ensure it is self-hosted or a wordpress.com instance where customization is possible.
Step 2: Navigate to the site's Customizer (e.g., <TARGET_URL>/wp-admin/customize.php or via the dashboard).
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the 'Menus' section within the WordPress Customizer.
Step 2: Select or add a 'Social Media' menu item (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, Instagram).
Step 3: In the 'Navigation Label' field for the selected social media link, input a Stored XSS payload.
Step 4: Click 'Publish' to save the changes.
Step 5: Visit the target WordPress site's homepage to observe the stored XSS payload execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests, though not strictly necessary for this PoC)
Manual browser interaction
success_indicators: An alert box pops up with the domain of the website when visiting the homepage.
The injected script is rendered and executed in the victim's browser when they view the site.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears and the input is displayed as plain text or is stripped → The input is properly sanitized, try different XSS payloads or check if this specific input field is vulnerable.
A 403 Forbidden error is encountered when trying to save the changes → The server-side WAF might be blocking the payload, try obfuscated payloads or alternative XSS vectors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the browser of any user who views the affected WordPress website. This can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or further exploitation depending on the privileges of the user viewing the page.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-provided input, especially in features that allow custom labels or text, to prevent the injection of executable code like XSS payloads.

meta.title: Cross Site Scripting (XSS) Reflected in one of the subdomains of “General Motors”

tech_stack_fingerprints: appears to be an old website

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain of the target organization is identified and appears to be an older website, suggesting potentially weaker input validation.
prerequisites: Ability to discover subdomains of the target organization.
applicability_signals: Subdomain identified (e.g., supply.eur.gm.com)
Website appears old or unmaintained
URL parameters are reflected in error messages or page content without proper sanitization.
skip_if: All subdomains are modern and appear to have robust input validation.
A WAF consistently blocks common XSS payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Sublist3r or a similar tool to enumerate subdomains for gm.com.
Step 2: Manually inspect discovered subdomains, prioritizing older-looking or less commonly used ones.
Step 3: For promising subdomains, test for common vulnerabilities like directory traversal and SQL injection to understand error handling.
Step 4: Observe if forcing an error or submitting malformed input causes URL content to be reflected in the response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a URL parameter on the target subdomain (e.g., supply.eur.gm.com).
Step 2: Construct an XSS payload, such as '', and URL-encode it.
Step 3: Inject the encoded payload into the identified URL parameter.
Step 4: Send the crafted request to the target subdomain.
Step 5: Observe the response for the execution of the JavaScript payload (e.g., an alert box).
tools_recommended: Sublist3r: For subdomain enumeration.
Burp Suite/OWASP ZAP: To intercept requests, modify parameters, and observe responses.
success_indicators: An alert box with the text 'XSS' appears in the browser.
The browser is redirected to a specified URL (e.g., www.google.com) via the `window.location.replace` payload.
failure_indicators: The payload is sanitized and rendered as plain text.
A 4xx or 5xx error code is returned without payload execution.
No visual indication of JavaScript execution (e.g., no alert box).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript code into the victim's browser, enabling actions such as session hijacking, phishing, credential theft, or redirecting users to malicious websites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect subdomain assets for input validation flaws, especially on older or less frequently updated websites, as they may represent a larger attack surface.

meta.title: Leaking Browser URL/Protocol Handlers

tech_stack_fingerprints: User-Agent string indicating Chrome, Firefox, or Edge

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target is using a vulnerable version of Firefox, Chrome, or Edge, and the attacker can control content displayed within the browser, such as through a malicious website.
prerequisites: Target is using a vulnerable version of Firefox, Chrome, or Edge.
Attacker can host a web page that the victim visits.
applicability_signals: Browser's handling of non-HTTP URI schemes.
Observable differences in image element rendering based on the URI scheme.
skip_if: Browser does not render images with different dimensions based on URI handlers.
Target is using an updated browser version that has patched the vulnerability.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target browser and version if possible through User-Agent or other means.
Step 2: If no specific version is known, proceed with the attack as it may be effective against multiple versions.
attack_steps: Step 1: Host a malicious HTML page on a web server.
Step 2: Include an `` tag on the HTML page where the `src` attribute is set to a protocol handler that may or may not be installed on the victim's system (e.g., `msteams://`, `spotify://`, etc.).
Step 3: Use JavaScript to dynamically measure the dimensions of the rendered `` element.

Step 4: Observe the rendered dimensions: a 24x24 pixel size typically indicates a non-existent handler (in Firefox, based on writeup), while a 0x0 size indicates an existing handler. Note: The writeup specifically details this for Firefox; Chrome and Edge behavior may differ and requires further testing.
Step 5: Iterate through a list of known protocol handlers and record which ones are present based on the measured image dimensions.
Step 6: Analyze the list of detected installed applications and security products.
tools_recommended: Web server (e.g., Nginx, Apache) to host the malicious HTML page.
Browser developer tools to inspect element dimensions and JavaScript console output.
A list of common protocol handlers for various applications.
success_indicators: Observable difference in the rendered `` element dimensions (e.g., 24x24 vs. 0x0 in Firefox) when switching between known and unknown protocol handlers.
JavaScript logs indicating the presence or absence of specific protocol handlers.
failure_indicators: No observable difference in image dimensions for any tested protocol handlers, suggesting the browser version is patched or the technique is ineffective on this specific browser.
All tested protocol handlers are reported as non-existent, even common ones, indicating a potential issue with the testing script or environment.

playbooks.impact: Leaking information about installed applications, including security products, which can be used by an attacker for targeted reconnaissance and more effective exploitation planning.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate how browsers handle non-HTTP URI schemes and if rendering differences can be leveraged to infer the presence of installed applications.

meta.title: An iOS zero-click radio proximity exploit odyssey

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting iOS devices with AWDL enabled and within radio proximity. The vulnerability is triggered without user interaction.
prerequisites: Target devices must be running a vulnerable version of iOS.
Target devices must have AWDL enabled.
Attacker must be within radio proximity of the target devices.
applicability_signals: Presence of AWDL communication between devices.
Recent iOS versions (pre-privacy enhancements).
skip_if: Target devices are running patched iOS versions.
AWDL is explicitly disabled on target devices.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target iOS devices in radio proximity.
Step 2: Confirm if AWDL is active on target devices by observing network traffic patterns or device settings.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft and send specially malformed data over AWDL to trigger a buffer overflow in the kernel's parsing of untrusted data.
Step 2: Exploit the memory corruption to gain arbitrary code execution in the kernel.
Step 3: Escalate privileges to steal user data.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup (requires crafting specific kernel-level exploit payloads targeting AWDL parsing vulnerabilities).
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup (likely requires custom tooling for AWDL packet manipulation, kernel exploit development, and reverse engineering of iOS kernel and AWDL protocol).
success_indicators: Target iOS devices rebooting without user interaction.
Successful arbitrary code execution on target devices.
Access to user data (photos, emails, messages).
failure_indicators: No observable reaction on target devices after sending crafted packets → investigate AWDL packet construction and kernel vulnerability trigger conditions.
Detection or blocking of malicious packets by iOS security mechanisms → refine exploit payload and delivery method.

playbooks.impact: Gain complete control over any nearby iPhone, allowing access to all user data including photos, emails, private messages, and real-time monitoring.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate kernel-level vulnerabilities in core device communication protocols like AWDL, as they can lead to wormable, zero-click exploits with severe impact.

meta.title: AliExpress Captcha Reuse

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target's login form utilizes predictable or reusable captcha images that can be enumerated or identified through indirect means (e.g., image size).
prerequisites: Target exposes a captcha endpoint that does not require authentication.
Captcha images are generated from a limited set or have predictable characteristics.
applicability_signals: Captcha images received from the same endpoint have consistent characteristics (e.g., file size, color depth, resolution).
Captcha images can be downloaded and analyzed without authentication.
skip_if: Captcha endpoint requires valid session cookies or tokens.
Captcha images are dynamically generated with high entropy and lack discernible patterns.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send unauthenticated GET requests to the captcha endpoint (e.g., /captcha/image/get.jsonp) with varying or dummy parameters (sessionid, identity, style) to enumerate available captcha images.
Step 2: Analyze the response headers and content type to confirm image data.
Step 3: Download and visually inspect multiple captcha images. Note any observed patterns or predictability in characters and image properties (e.g., size, color).
attack_steps: Step 1: Collect a set of captcha images by repeatedly requesting them from the captcha endpoint.
Step 2: For each collected captcha image, determine an indexing mechanism. The writeup suggests using the file size of the image after the JPG EOI (End Of Image) marker.
Step 3: Create a mapping (e.g., a dictionary or hash map) from the determined index (e.g., image size) to the actual captcha text.
Step 4: During a login attempt, request a captcha, determine its index (e.g., size), and use the pre-computed mapping to retrieve the correct captcha answer.
Step 5: Submit the login form with the retrieved captcha answer.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET /captcha/image/get.jsonp?sessionid=random&identity=data&style=default&callback=callback HTTP/1.1
Host: usdiablo.alibaba.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64; rv:83.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/83.0
Accept: */*
Accept-Language: fr,fr-FR;q=0.8,en-US;q=0.5,en;q=0.3
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Connection: close
Referer: https://www.aliexpress.com/

(Analyze response body for image data. Calculate image size and map to known captcha text.)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and replay HTTP requests, and to analyze response content.
Python with Pillow library: For image manipulation, size extraction, and creating the mapping.
Tesseract OCR: For initial analysis or if image size mapping proves insufficient.
success_indicators: Successful login without solving the captcha manually.
The login form accepts a pre-determined captcha answer based on the derived index.
The target system does not return an invalid captcha error.
failure_indicators: Login attempt fails with an invalid captcha error, indicating the bypass was unsuccessful.
The server enforces stricter, per-session captcha generation or validation, making the index-based mapping invalid.
A different parameter like 'captchaToken' is required and not predictable, as hinted in the writeup.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass the captcha mechanism during login, potentially enabling automated brute-force attacks, account enumeration, or other credential stuffing activities if the captcha was intended to prevent such actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate captcha mechanisms for predictability by analyzing image properties or response characteristics, as relying solely on visual randomness can be a weakness.

meta.title: Bcrypt — Account TakeOver Due To Weak Encryption — #HR51KDB

tech_stack_fingerprints: OTP sent to registered mobile number, response contains 'otpPassword' field with Bcrypt hash

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a 'OTP Login' feature is present and the OTP is sent to a registered mobile number, and it's observed that the OTP is leaked in the response body in an encrypted format (Bcrypt).
prerequisites: Attacker has registered an account and possesses valid login credentials.
applicability_signals: The login process involves sending an OTP to a registered mobile number.
Intercepting the 'OTP Login' request and observing 'otpPassword' field in the response body containing a Bcrypt hash.
skip_if: OTP is not leaked in the response.
OTP is not in Bcrypt format or is not extractable.
The OTP login functionality does not exist or is not vulnerable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify and interact with the OTP Login functionality on the target application.
Step 2: Intercept the HTTP request/response for the OTP Login process using a proxy like Burp Suite.
attack_steps: Step 1: Perform the OTP Login for a known registered account.
Step 2: Analyze the response body for any leaked OTP, specifically looking for a field like 'otpPassword' containing a Bcrypt hash.
Step 3: Copy the Bcrypt encrypted OTP value.
Step 4: Use a tool like 'Debcrypt' (https://github.com/BREAKTEAM/Debcrypt) to decrypt the Bcrypt hash.
Step 5: If decryption is successful, use the decrypted OTP to log into the victim's account.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses.
Debcrypt (https://github.com/BREAKTEAM/Debcrypt): To decrypt Bcrypt hashes.
success_indicators: Successful decryption of the Bcrypt hash to reveal the actual OTP.
Successful login to the victim's account using the decrypted OTP.
Account Takeover is confirmed.
failure_indicators: No 'otpPassword' field found in the response.
The 'otpPassword' field does not contain a valid Bcrypt hash format.
Decryption of the Bcrypt hash fails or results in an invalid OTP.
The decrypted OTP does not grant access to the account.

playbooks.impact: Account Takeover of victim's account, leading to unauthorized access to sensitive information, data manipulation, or further malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check for sensitive information leakage in API responses, especially during authentication flows. Pay close attention to encrypted or hashed values, as weak encryption or implementation flaws can lead to bypasses.

meta.title: The Story of my first critical bug

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website has a search bar where user input is reflected in the response page, and the backend technology is suspected to be ASP.NET which might use MSSQL.
prerequisites: Target has a search functionality that reflects input in the response.
applicability_signals: Search bar present on the website.
User input in the search bar is rendered in the response page.
skip_if: WAF blocks SQLi payloads.
No search functionality is present or input is not reflected.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse to https://subdomain.redacted.com/ and identify the search bar.
Step 2: Submit simple search queries (e.g., 'test', 'abc') and observe if the input is reflected in the response page.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send GET request to https://subdomain.redacted.com/?search=' or 1=1 --
Step 2: Send GET request to https://subdomain.redacted.com/?search=' or 1=2 --
Step 3: Intercept the request with Burp Suite and send the search payload to Burp Repeater.
Step 4: Modify the search parameter with the blind SQLi time-based payload: ' WAITFOR DELAY '00:00:10'
Step 5: Observe the response time. If it is around 10 seconds, increment the delay (e.g., '00:00:20') and re-test.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater for precise timing)
Browser developer tools (for initial observation)
success_indicators: Noticeable delay in the server response corresponding to the specified delay in the payload (e.g., ~10 seconds for '00:00:10').
failure_indicators: No significant delay observed in server response for time-based payloads. Consider if a WAF is blocking or if the RDBMS is not MSSQL.
Immediate 403 or similar error response might indicate WAF protection. Pivot to techniques that evade WAFs or target different vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can extract data from the database using blind SQL injection techniques, potentially leading to information disclosure, unauthorized access, or further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for blind SQL injection, especially time-based payloads, on search functionalities where user input is reflected, even if the technology stack appears robust.

meta.title: Fixing a Google Vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has the 'dataproc.clusters.create' permission (or equivalent in other services like Dataflow) in a GCP project and is able to create resources without proper privilege checks.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated GCP account.
Attacker has permissions to create Dataproc clusters (or similar resources).
applicability_signals: User is able to create Dataproc clusters.
When creating a Dataproc cluster, the created resource is automatically assigned the 'Project Editor' role without explicit configuration.
skip_if: The GCP environment strictly enforces least privilege, and resource creation permissions do not grant elevated roles by default.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify available GCP services and user permissions using GCP CLI or console.
Step 2: Check if 'dataproc.clusters.create' permission (or equivalent for other services like Dataflow) is granted to the user.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute `gcloud dataproc clusters create <cluster-name> --region=<region> --project=<project-id>` (or equivalent API call) without specifying service account or IAM bindings.
Step 2: Once the cluster is created, observe the IAM roles assigned to the service account associated with the cluster.

Step 3: If the cluster's service account has been implicitly granted elevated privileges (e.g., 'Project Editor' role), use these escalated privileges to perform actions that were previously restricted.
tools_recommended: gcloud CLI for interacting with GCP services
Google Cloud Console for observing IAM roles and permissions
success_indicators: The service account for the newly created Dataproc cluster is observed to have the 'Project Editor' role (or equivalent higher privilege role).
Attacker is able to perform actions with the escalated privileges that were previously denied.
failure_indicators: The created cluster's service account does not receive elevated IAM roles beyond what was explicitly granted.
Attempts to perform actions with the service account's permissions are still restricted as per the initial user's permissions. This indicates the default settings are not exploitable in this manner.

playbooks.impact: An attacker with the ability to create Dataproc clusters (or similar resources) can escalate their privileges to 'Project Editor' or equivalent, gaining broad administrative access to the GCP project, enabling lateral movement, data exfiltration, and full project compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always review and audit the default IAM roles and permissions granted to service accounts when creating cloud resources, as unintended privilege escalation can occur if not explicitly configured for least privilege.

meta.title: Weird (im)possible XSS on error page

tech_stack_fingerprints: Redirects to homepage on invalid input
Error pages are integrated into the website

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application exhibits error pages that are integrated into the main website and can be triggered by appending file extensions to URLs, and these error pages reflect user input.
prerequisites: The ability to trigger an error page by appending file extensions (e.g., .php, .aspx) to a known URL.
applicability_signals: Appending `.php` or `.aspx` to a path like `/test` results in an error page.
The error page reflects user-controlled input.
The error page redirects to the homepage or a specific security detection page when certain payloads are introduced.
skip_if: The error page is entirely disabled or shows a generic, non-reflective error message.
All attempted XSS payloads are completely blocked or sanitized without any reflection.

recon_steps: Attempt to trigger error pages by appending common file extensions (.php, .aspx, .jsp, .html) to various directories and paths on the target application.
Analyze the response for reflected user input within the error message or page content.
attack_steps: Identify a URL that triggers an error page when a file extension is appended (e.g., `<TARGET_URL>/test.aspx`).
Craft a payload that includes an XSS vector and is appended as part of the filename, ending with an executable extension (e.g., `.aspx`).
The key is to inject the payload in a way that bypasses the security detection mechanism, potentially by not closing tags or by manipulating the filename itself.
Example payload structure: `%3Cscript%3Ealert(1)` followed by an executable extension like `.aspx` or similar.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To test various payloads against the error page.
URL Encoder/Decoder: To properly format payloads when they are part of a filename.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code (e.g., an `alert()` box) within the context of the error page, even if the page itself is not the primary target.
The reflected payload, or parts of it, appear in the response without being fully sanitized.
failure_indicators: The error page redirects to the homepage or a security detection page without any JavaScript execution.
The injected payload is completely stripped or encoded, preventing any form of execution.
The target application returns a 403 Forbidden error for requests attempting to use payloads as filenames.

playbooks.impact: Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser when they visit the specially crafted URL that triggers the error page.
playbooks.key_lesson: When error pages reflect user input, especially when triggered by file extension manipulation, carefully test XSS payloads that might bypass security filters by being part of a filename or by exploiting tag-closing vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Exploiting dynamic rendering engines to take control of web apps

target_profile.technology: Dynamic Rendering Engines (e.g., Next.js, Nuxt.js, etc.)
affected_components: Endpoints that render dynamic content client-side or server-side
tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML containing `<script>` tags with dynamic data (e.g., `__NEXT_DATA__` for Next.js, `window.__NUXT__` for Nuxt.js)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application utilizes a dynamic rendering engine that fetches or processes external resources based on user input or configuration, and the application fails to properly validate or sanitize these resources before rendering.
prerequisites: The target web application uses a framework that relies on dynamic rendering (e.g., Next.js, Nuxt.js).
The application exposes endpoints or functionality that takes external resource URLs or identifiers as input, which are then processed by the rendering engine.
applicability_signals: Presence of `__NEXT_DATA__` or similar global JavaScript variables in the HTML source.
Endpoints that appear to be rendering content from external sources without explicit validation.
skip_if: All external resource fetching is strictly controlled and validated against an allowlist.
The rendering engine is sandboxed and cannot initiate outbound network requests.
WAF blocks requests to common SSRF endpoints or payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the HTML source code of the target web application for clues about the rendering engine (e.g., `__NEXT_DATA__`, framework-specific script tags).
Step 2: Identify endpoints or parameters that seem to accept URLs or references to external content.
Step 3: Examine how these external resources are fetched and rendered. Look for instances where the application fetches content based on user-supplied URLs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a payload that points to an internal or sensitive IP address (e.g., `http://127.0.0.1:8080` or `http://169.254.169.254`).
Step 2: Submit this payload through the identified vulnerable endpoint or parameter.
Step 3: Observe the application's response. If the rendering engine attempts to fetch the resource, it may reveal information about the internal network or cloud metadata services.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater for manual testing, Intruder for brute-forcing if applicable)
curl (for quick manual checks)
Nmap (to probe internal ports if SSRF is confirmed)
success_indicators: Response contains data from an internal service.
Response contains metadata from cloud provider instance metadata endpoints.
Application behavior changes in a way that suggests it's attempting to connect to a specific internal resource.
failure_indicators: 400 or 403 errors indicating validation failed.
No observable change in behavior or response content when submitting internal URLs.
Response indicates that the URL is being fetched but returns an error code from the target URL itself rather than internal data (e.g., 404 from an internal service).

playbooks.impact: Sensitive information disclosure (e.g., cloud instance metadata, internal service configurations), potential for further exploitation by chaining SSRF with other vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with dynamic rendering engines, always validate and sanitize any user-supplied external resource URLs or identifiers to prevent SSRF vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Bypassing the Redirect filters with 7 ways

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an OAuth endpoint accepts a redirect URL parameter that is not properly sanitized, allowing for open redirects.
prerequisites: Target application uses OAuth 2.0.
Attacker has a controllable domain for receiving tokens.
applicability_signals: Presence of an OAuth endpoint.
The OAuth endpoint accepts a `redirect` or `next` URL parameter.
Initial attempts at standard open redirect payloads fail, suggesting filter bypasses are needed.
skip_if: The OAuth endpoint strictly validates redirect URIs against a whitelist and does not show any signs of filter bypass vulnerabilities.
All requests to the OAuth endpoint result in immediate errors without processing the redirect parameter.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify OAuth endpoints and parameters in the target application. Look for parameters like `redirect_uri`, `redirect`, `next`, `return_to`, etc. Use tools like Burp Suite's scanner or manual inspection.
Step 2: Test basic open redirect payloads in the identified parameters to see if any are effective. (e.g., `https://www.target01.com/api/OAUTH?next=https://attacker.com`)
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to bypass redirect filters using various encoding and special character techniques.

Step 2: For Path-URI bypass, append `//attacker.com` to a valid subdomain of the target, if the filter accepts any path with a valid host. Example: `https://www.target01.com/api/OAUTH/?next=https://www.target01.com//mahdi.com/`
Step 3: For `%09` (tab character) bypass, URL-encode a tab character within the redirect URL. Example: `https://www.target01.com/api/OAUTH?next=https://www.target01.com%09.mahdi.com`
Step 4: For double URL encoding of dot (`%252E`) bypass, use `%252E` instead of `.` in the redirect URL. Example: `https://www.target01.com/api/OAUTH/?next=https://www.target01.com%252e.mahdi.com`

Step 5: For filter bypass using `[%5b]` (URL-encoded left bracket), try injecting this character into the redirect URL and see if it allows for subdomain takeovers or other manipulations. Example: `https://target02.com/api/oauth?code=&state=https://Mahdi.com$FUZZ$.target02.com/` (Note: This example is incomplete from the writeup)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: For fuzzing URL-encoded characters and common bypass techniques.
Google Chrome DevTools: To observe redirect chains and analyze network requests.
success_indicators: The victim is redirected to `attacker.com` with their OAuth token and email in the URL parameters (e.g., `https://attacker.com/?token=xxx&email=...`).
A redirect to a subdomain of `attacker.com` that is controlled by the attacker, effectively exfiltrating the token.
failure_indicators: The target application returns an error indicating an invalid redirect URL or shows no redirection behavior. -> Pivot to other open redirect bypass techniques or entirely different vulnerabilities.
The filter correctly blocks the payload, returning a standard error message or redirecting to a safe page. -> Try different bypass strings or focus on other parameters/endpoints.

playbooks.impact: Account Takeover (ATO) via token theft. If the OAuth flow is used for authentication or authorization, stealing the token allows the attacker to impersonate the victim user. In some cases, it can lead to accessing sensitive user data or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing open redirects, especially in OAuth flows, go beyond standard payloads and investigate various encoding techniques, special characters (%09, %252e), and path-based bypasses to circumvent filters.

meta.title: Out of Band XXE in an E-commerce IOS app

affected_components: API endpoints that accept JSON but process XML internally
tech_stack_fingerprints: XML parsing error displayed to end user

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an API endpoint that typically accepts JSON returns an XML parsing error upon receiving a generic XML payload, indicating that the server attempts to process XML.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to intercept and modify HTTP requests sent from the iOS app.
Attacker must have a listener server (e.g., using ngrok, Burp Collaborator, or a custom server) to receive out-of-band data.
applicability_signals: API endpoint returns an XML parsing error when a malformed XML is sent.
API endpoint accepts XML content type.
skip_if: WAF blocks all XML payloads.
API strictly validates content types and rejects non-JSON requests without any XML parsing attempt.

recon_steps: Step 1: Intercept an API request from the iOS app.
Step 2: Modify the 'Content-Type' header to 'application/xml' and send a generic XML payload (e.g., '<test></test>'). Observe the response for XML parsing errors.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up an out-of-band listener (e.g., XXEserve, Burp Collaborator).
Step 2: Craft an XXE payload to request a specific file (e.g., '/etc/passwd', '/etc/resolv.conf') from the server and send it to the listener's URL.
Step 3: Construct the final XML payload with the DOCTYPE declaration referencing the external entity pointing to the attacker's listener, and embed the file path to be read.
Step 4: Send the crafted XML payload to the vulnerable API endpoint, ensuring the Content-Type is set to 'application/xml'.
Step 5: Monitor the attacker's listener for incoming requests containing the exfiltrated file content.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting, modifying, and replaying HTTP requests.
XXEserve or similar OOB XXE tools: To host a web server and capture exfiltrated data.
Ngrok/Burp Collaborator: To expose a local listener to the internet.
success_indicators: The attacker's listener receives an HTTP request from the target IP address containing the content of the requested file (e.g., /etc/resolv.conf).
Response from the target server includes the exfiltrated data within an error message or directly in the response body (less common for OOB XXE).
failure_indicators: No connection is received on the attacker's listener. → The XXE payload is not being processed correctly or the server is not making outbound requests.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is received, and the listener doesn't receive any request. → Potential WAF blocking the request or malformed XML.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can read sensitive files from the server's filesystem (e.g., configuration files, credentials, application source code), perform SSRF to scan internal networks, and potentially achieve RCE if sensitive information is disclosed.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always disable external entity processing in XML parsers and validate XML input strictly. For JSON endpoints, ensure XML parsing is disabled entirely if not explicitly required.

meta.title: GraphQL IDOR in Facebook streamer dashboard

affected_components: GraphQL endpoint for streamer dashboard data

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access streamer dashboard data via GraphQL, and the `profile_id` parameter is used.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Facebook account.
applicability_signals: GraphQL requests originating from facebook.com/gaming/streamer
GraphQL query named 'GamesVideoStreamerDashboardProfileQuery'
Presence of a `profile_id` parameter in the GraphQL request body.
skip_if: GraphQL requests do not contain a `profile_id` parameter or are not related to the streamer dashboard.
The `profile_id` parameter is not user-controllable or is properly validated against the authenticated user's page ownership.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to `https://www.facebook.com/gaming/streamer` to access the Creator Studio dashboard.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite to intercept all outgoing requests from the Creator Studio. Search for requests containing the GraphQL query `GamesVideoStreamerDashboardProfileQuery`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Forward the intercepted request containing `GamesVideoStreamerDashboardProfileQuery` to Burp Suite Repeater.
Step 2: In Repeater, modify the `profile_id` parameter in the GraphQL request body to a known Facebook page ID of another streamer.
Step 3: Send the modified request and analyze the response for sensitive data that does not belong to the attacker's account.

payloads_or_patterns.template: query GamesVideoStreamerDashboardProfileQuery($profile_id: ID!) { viewer { profile(id: $profile_id) { games_streamer_dashboard { l30_live_earnings supporter_count } } } }

tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Intruder for enumerating profile_ids if feasible)
success_indicators: Response contains sensitive data such as `l30_live_earnings` and `supporter_count` belonging to another Facebook page.
The attacker can view dashboard stats for pages they do not administer.
failure_indicators: Response indicates an authorization error (e.g., 403 Forbidden) or the sensitive fields are null/empty for other page IDs.
The `profile_id` parameter is not present or is not the source of the vulnerability.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can access sensitive financial and engagement data (live earnings, supporter counts) of other Facebook streamers without authorization, potentially leading to competitive disadvantage or exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that `profile_id` parameters in GraphQL queries are strictly scoped to the authenticated user's authorized resources, especially when sensitive data is involved.

meta.title: OpenEMR 5.0.1.3 Arbitrary File Actions
meta.vuln_type: Arbitrary File Read | Arbitrary File Write | Arbitrary File Delete

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can interact with the /portal/import_template.php script and the target version is vulnerable (e.g., OpenEMR 5.0.1.3).
prerequisites: Attacker must have valid user credentials to log into OpenEMR.
applicability_signals: The presence of the /portal/import_template.php endpoint.
The target system responds to POST requests on this endpoint.
skip_if: If all requests to /portal/import_template.php with different modes result in errors or unexpected behavior not related to file operations.
If the server rejects POST requests to the endpoint entirely.

recon_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access the /portal/import_template.php endpoint to confirm its existence and response characteristics.
Step 2: Verify that the target version is indeed OpenEMR 5.0.1.3 or an earlier unpatched version by checking version information (if available) or known CVEs.
attack_steps: Step 1 (Arbitrary File Read): Send a POST request to /portal/import_template.php with the following payload: {'mode': 'get', 'docid': '/etc/passwd'}. Observe the response for file content.

Step 2 (Arbitrary File Write): Send a POST request to /portal/import_template.php with the following payload: {'mode': 'save', 'docid': 'backdoor.php', 'content': '<?php phpinfo(); ?>'}. Observe the response for success.
Step 3 (Arbitrary File Delete): Send a POST request to /portal/import_template.php with the following payload: {'mode': 'delete', 'docid': 'backdoor.php'}. Observe the response for success.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send custom POST requests to /portal/import_template.php with varying 'mode' and 'docid' parameters.
success_indicators: Arbitrary File Read: The response body contains the content of the requested file (e.g., /etc/passwd).
Arbitrary File Write: The server responds with 'true' or a success indicator, and subsequent attempts to read the written file are successful.
Arbitrary File Delete: The server responds with 'true' or a success indicator, and subsequent attempts to read the deleted file result in a file not found error.
failure_indicators: Any mode parameter other than 'get', 'save', or 'delete' results in an error or unexpected behavior. → Focus on the other modes.
POST requests to /portal/import_template.php are blocked or return 403. → This technique is not applicable.
Response does not contain expected file content for 'get' mode. → The file path may be incorrect, or sanitization is in place.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can read arbitrary files from the server's filesystem, write arbitrary files (including web shells) to the filesystem, and delete arbitrary files. Combined with the file write, this leads to Remote Code Execution.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with scripts that perform file operations (read, write, delete), always test for unsanitized user input in file path parameters, especially when specific 'mode' parameters control the operation.

meta.title: Stealing User’s PII info by visiting API endpoint directly

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can initiate an order and send a payment link to a customer, and the API endpoint for handling customer details can be accessed directly or by other users.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to the 'Place order' feature or can generate similar payment links.
applicability_signals: The API endpoint `POST /na/customer/client/v1/session/<session_id>` is observed handling customer details.
Session IDs are present in the URL.
skip_if: The API endpoint strictly validates session ownership or user roles.
No customer details are returned or accessible by unauthorized users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Observe network traffic when a seller sends a 'Place order' feature to a customer and intercepts the requests made when the customer visits the payment link.
Step 2: Identify the API endpoint used to submit customer details and note the structure of the session ID in the URL.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a payment link for a different customer. This can be done by initiating an order as a seller and obtaining the generated payment link.

Step 2: Directly visit the payment link or use a tool like Burp Suite to modify the request to the API endpoint identified in recon, substituting the session ID with the one from the other customer's payment link.
Step 3: Submit the form with arbitrary or pre-filled details.
Step 4: Analyze the API response to check if customer PII from the targeted user is leaked or accessible through the endpoint.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /na/customer/client/v1/session/<OTHER_USER_SESSION_ID> HTTP/1.1
Host: payments-na.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10.15; rv:78.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/78.0
Accept: application/json, text/plain, */*
Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.5
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate
Content-Type: application/json;charset=utf-8
X-Cdc-Client: 2.15.45
Origin: https://payments-na.example.com
DNT: 1
Connection: close
Referer: https://payments-na.example.com

{"browser_prefilled":[],"customer_details":{"country":"US"..............},"skipped_fields":[],"user_submitted":true,"action":"continueAddressUnverified"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, specifically changing the session ID in the API endpoint.
success_indicators: The API response contains PII (e.g., name, email, address) of another user whose payment link was targeted.
An observable change in the returned data suggests access to unauthorized user information.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden error for the modified session ID suggests strong access control.
Receiving the same user's data or an error related to invalid session indicates the session ID is not the sole differentiator or is strictly validated.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can access and disclose Personally Identifiable Information (PII) of any user who has initiated a payment or order process, leading to privacy violations and potential identity theft.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test API endpoints that handle user session data by attempting to access them with session identifiers belonging to other users.

meta.title: Optimizing Hunting Results in VDP for use in Bug Bounty Programs - From Sensitive Information Disclosure to Accessing Hidden APIs which can be used to Retrieve Customer Data
meta.vuln_type: IDOR | Information Disclosure | Broken Access Control | SQL Injection

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When sensitive information like user emails, credentials, or API endpoints can be discovered through VDP programs or other informational disclosures on related subdomains, and these disclosures can be chained with vulnerabilities on the main bounty target.
prerequisites: Attacker can discover sensitive information (e.g., credentials, endpoints) via VDP programs or other means.
Target has multiple subdomains, some of which may be points-only programs.
Target has vulnerabilities like SQL Injection, IDOR, or Information Disclosure on subdomains or the main target.
applicability_signals: Discovery of user emails and credentials from an unprotected Jira instance.
Existence of user enumeration features.
Presence of SQL Injection vulnerabilities that allow email enumeration.
IDOR vulnerabilities exposing administrator dashboards or user data.
Sensitive information disclosure via debug modes.
Hidden APIs or undocumented API endpoints.
skip_if: No sensitive information or useful endpoints can be found through VDP or initial reconnaissance.
All discovered vulnerabilities on subdomains do not provide actionable intelligence for the main bounty target.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan subdomains of the target for open or unprotected services like Jira. Look for credentials, discussions, and discovered endpoints within the Jira instance.
Step 2: Identify and exploit user enumeration functionalities to gather a list of registered email addresses. This can involve simple API calls or forms.
Step 3: Test for SQL Injection vulnerabilities on various endpoints, particularly those that handle user-related data, to enumerate all registered email addresses.
Step 4: Investigate IDOR vulnerabilities by attempting to access other users' data or administrative panels using different user accounts or by manipulating IDs.
Step 5: Probe for sensitive information disclosure, especially through debug modes or verbose error messages, to obtain internal employee email lists or configuration details.
Step 6: Analyze API traffic (using intercepted requests from development areas or general API interaction) to discover hidden or undocumented API endpoints.
Step 7: Collect and save all discovered information (emails, credentials, endpoints, API details) for potential chaining.
attack_steps: Step 1: Leverage information obtained from an unprotected Jira instance (May 2018) on sub1.target.tld, which provided email addresses, credentials, and endpoints, to aid in attacks against mainredacted.target.tld.
Step 2: Utilize email addresses enumerated from user enumeration issues (August 2019) on sub2.target.tld and SQL Injection issues (August 2019) on sub3.target.tld.
Step 3: Exploit an IDOR vulnerability (August 2019) on sub4.target.tld to gain access to administrator dashboards and retrieve a comprehensive list of registered email addresses.
Step 4: Combine information from the unprotected Jira instance (May 2018) with other discovered vulnerabilities to exploit a Broken Access Control issue (October 2019) on mainredacted.target.tld.
Step 5: Use sensitive information disclosure (December 2019) via debug mode on targetXYZ.tld to access administrator dashboards and download a CSV file containing internal employee email addresses.

Step 6: In September 2020, chain issues including weak login implementation, weak credentials (identified from previous activity), the use of hidden APIs discovered earlier (January 2020), and rate limit issues to achieve a P1 exploit on mainredacted.target.tld.
payloads_or_patterns: email enumeration queries for SQLi: ' UNION SELECT 1, email, null FROM users --
IDOR manipulation: Change user ID parameters (e.g., /user/<ID>, /profile?userId=<ID>)
Hidden API endpoints discovered through traffic analysis.
Payloads related to weak login implementations and credential stuffing.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Intruder, Scanner)
Subfinder / Amass for subdomain enumeration
SQLMap for SQL Injection testing
Postman or curl for API interaction
Jira (for initial reconnaissance if accessible)
success_indicators: Successful enumeration of user emails and credentials.
Access to administrator dashboards or sensitive user data via IDOR.
Successful exploitation of Broken Access Control, leading to unauthorized access.
Retrieval of sensitive files (e.g., CSV of employee emails).
Successful exploitation of chained vulnerabilities resulting in RCE or significant data access.
HTTP responses indicating access to unauthorized resources or data.
failure_indicators: All SQLi attempts return no relevant data or generic errors.
IDOR attempts result in 'Access Denied' or redirect to login.
API requests to potential hidden endpoints return 404 or 403.
WAF blocks common SQLi or XSS payloads.

playbooks.impact: Retrieval of sensitive customer and internal employee data, unauthorized access to administrative dashboards, potential for further exploitation leading to RCE or full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always correlate findings from VDP programs and lower-impact bug types (like information disclosure) with your primary targets, as they can provide crucial intelligence for chaining vulnerabilities and achieving higher impact bugs.

meta.title: Microsoft Bug Bounty Writeup – Stored XSS Vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows users to submit and share content that is stored and later displayed to other users, and input sanitization is insufficient.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to register an account on the target application.
applicability_signals: Presence of user-defined input fields that are stored persistently.
Feature allowing users to share their 'stories' or content.
skip_if: Input fields are thoroughly sanitized or escaped on both input and output.
Content sharing feature is disabled or not present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use subdomain enumeration tools (Knock, Subrake, Sublist3r, Amass) to discover subdomains of Microsoft.
Step 2: Identify subdomains that appear to host user-generated content or have registration/login functionalities (e.g., storybook.office.com).
Step 3: Register an account on the identified target subdomain to gain authenticated access.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the story sharing feature within the application.
Step 2: Identify input fields where user-defined content is accepted (e.g., title, description, content of a story).
Step 3: Craft a simple Stored XSS payload (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`).
Step 4: Inject the payload into one or more input fields where user content is stored.
Step 5: Save or submit the content.
Step 6: Navigate to the page where the shared content is displayed to other users.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests)
Subdomain enumeration tools (Knock, Subrake, Sublist3r, Amass)
success_indicators: The XSS payload executes in the browser of a user viewing the shared content, typically indicated by an alert box displaying the domain name.
failure_indicators: The payload is displayed as plain text or is stripped from the input/output, indicating effective sanitization. Pivot to testing different input fields or for other vulnerability types.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of other users' browsers who view the shared content. This can lead to session hijacking, phishing attacks, defacement, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test user-supplied input fields in features that store and display content to other users for Stored XSS vulnerabilities by injecting common XSS payloads.

meta.title: Evernote: Universal-XSS, theft of all cookies from all sites, and more

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an unexported Activity within the Android application accepts arbitrary HTML/JS content and a base URL that can include authentication cookies.
prerequisites: Attacker has the ability to launch intents targeting specific unexported activities within the Evernote app.
The target device has the Evernote app installed and the user is logged in.
applicability_signals: Presence of Activities like Widget4x1SettingsActivity or Widget4x2SettingsActivity that can launch other activities.
An unexported Activity (e.g., GnomeWebViewActivity) that accepts `EXTRA_BASE_URL` and `EXTRA_HTML_CONTENT` as Intent extras.
The target Activity uses `WebView.loadDataWithBaseURL()` with user-provided or manipulated parameters.
skip_if: The target Activities are exported and cannot be launched by a malicious app.
The `WebView.loadDataWithBaseURL()` implementation sanitizes HTML/JS input or does not include authentication cookies in the base URL.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the Evernote Android application's manifest file to identify exported and unexported Activities.
Step 2: Identify Activities that accept Intent extras related to web content or URLs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an Android Intent to launch `com.evernote.engine.gnome.GnomeWebViewActivity`.
Step 2: Set the `EXTRA_BASE_URL` parameter to a crafted URL that includes a malicious domain and injects authentication cookies.
Step 3: Set the `EXTRA_HTML_CONTENT` parameter to an HTML payload containing JavaScript, e.g., `<script>alert(document.cookie)</script>`.
Step 4: Execute the Intent, ideally from another malicious application installed on the same device.
tools_recommended: Android Debug Bridge (adb): To launch intents and interact with the application.
Frida/Objection: For dynamic analysis and to hook into the application's components.
success_indicators: The WebView loads the provided HTML and executes the JavaScript, e.g., an alert box showing `document.cookie`.
Network traffic originating from the Evernote app to a controlled server, potentially containing stolen cookies.
failure_indicators: The `GnomeWebViewActivity` crashes or fails to load the content.
No JavaScript execution or cookie exfiltration is observed.
The `loadDataWithBaseURL` method sanitizes the input or does not include cookies.

playbooks.impact: Universal XSS allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution in the context of the Evernote domain, leading to theft of all session cookies from all sites visited by the user within that WebView context. This can lead to full account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize how Android Activities handle sensitive data (like URLs and HTML content) passed via Intents, especially when they lead to WebView rendering, as authentication cookies can be inadvertently exposed.

meta.title: Local Privilege Escalation Vulnerability Discovered in VMware Fusion

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a standard user can execute applications from an untrusted location, leading to modifications of system-wide PATH variables during application startup.
prerequisites: Attacker has standard user privileges on the target system.
applicability_signals: VMware Fusion is installed and running on the target system.
The file /etc/paths.d/com.vmware.fusion.public exists.
skip_if: VMware Fusion is not installed.
The target system has strict file integrity controls preventing modification of /etc/paths.d.

recon_steps: Verify installation of VMware Fusion.
Check for the existence of /etc/paths.d/com.vmware.fusion.public.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a directory structure mimicking the VMware Fusion application in a user-controlled location (e.g., /Users/<username>/.vmware.stager/VMware Fusion.app/Contents/Public).
Step 2: Use hard links to copy the necessary parts of the original VMware Fusion application into the crafted directory structure.
Step 3: Execute the copied VMware Fusion application from the user-controlled location.
Step 4: Wait for the application to complete its startup processes (approximately 5 seconds).
Step 5: Observe that the /etc/paths.d/com.vmware.fusion.public file has been updated with the path to the crafted directory structure.
tools_recommended: Standard OS commands (cp, ln, rm, mkdir) for file manipulation and execution.
Custom script to automate the copying and execution process.
success_indicators: The file /etc/paths.d/com.vmware.fusion.public is modified to contain a path pointing to the attacker-controlled directory.
Subsequent interactive logins or execution of commands without full path specification result in the execution of malicious binaries from the attacker-controlled path.
failure_indicators: The file /etc/paths.d/com.vmware.fusion.public is not modified.
Executing the copied VMware Fusion application results in an error or unexpected behavior without modifying the PATH file.
The system prevents the modification of /etc/paths.d.

playbooks.impact: An attacker with standard user privileges can inject malicious code or binaries into the system's PATH environment variable, which can be executed with root privileges upon subsequent user logins or when applications are run without full path specification.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application dynamically updates system configuration files like PATH variables during startup, test if a user can influence these updates by executing a modified or copied version of the application.

meta.title: id.atlassian.com Username enumeration

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the `/rest/marketing-consent/config` API endpoint is accessible and accepts an email parameter.
prerequisites: Access to the internet to send HTTP requests to id.atlassian.com
applicability_signals: Endpoint /rest/marketing-consent/config exists
Endpoint accepts POST requests with JSON body containing an 'email' field
Response contains 'implicitConsent' field
skip_if: The /rest/marketing-consent/config endpoint is not found or returns an error for all requests.
The 'implicitConsent' field does not change based on valid/invalid email inputs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access https://id.atlassian.com/rest/marketing-consent/config endpoint to confirm its existence and response format.
Step 2: Send a POST request with a known valid email address (e.g., attacker-controlled domain) and observe the 'implicitConsent' value.
Step 3: Send a POST request with a known invalid email address and observe the 'implicitConsent' value. Confirm it differs from the valid email response.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain a list of potential usernames/email prefixes (e.g., from SecLists).
Step 2: Construct email addresses by combining prefixes with a common domain (e.g., @atlassian.com or a known Atlassian customer domain if identified).
Step 3: Iterate through the constructed email addresses and send POST requests to https://id.atlassian.com/rest/marketing-consent/config with each email in the JSON payload.
Step 4: Analyze the 'implicitConsent' field in the response for each request. 'false' indicates a registered Atlassian user, 'true' indicates an unregistered email.
Step 5: If rate limiting is observed (X-Ratelimit-* headers), implement delays in requests to stay within the limits (e.g., 100 requests per 60 seconds).
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /rest/marketing-consent/config HTTP/1.1
Host: id.atlassian.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"email": "<EMAIL_ADDRESS>"}
tools_recommended: curl: For sending individual requests and scripting.
Custom Python/Bash script: To automate the iteration over email lists, request sending, response parsing, and rate limit handling.
success_indicators: Receiving a JSON response with `"implicitConsent":false` for a given email address.
Receiving a JSON response with `"implicitConsent":true` for a different email address.
failure_indicators: Receiving consistent `"implicitConsent":true` for all tested emails (may indicate no users exist or a change in API behavior).
Receiving HTTP 429 Too Many Requests or consistent 403 Forbidden responses (indicates effective rate limiting or IP ban). Pivot: Use rotating proxies or distribute requests across multiple IPs.
API endpoint is unavailable or returns unexpected errors for all requests.

playbooks.impact: Enumeration of valid Atlassian user email addresses. This information can be used for targeted social engineering, credential stuffing attacks, or further reconnaissance.
playbooks.key_lesson: Test API endpoints that accept email addresses or usernames for username enumeration vulnerabilities by observing differences in responses for valid and invalid inputs.

meta.title: 31k$ SSRF in Google Cloud Monitoring led to metadata exposure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target offers a feature to create uptime checks or periodic requests to external resources, and allows custom headers.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Google Cloud account.
applicability_signals: Existence of 'Uptime Checks' feature within Google Cloud Monitoring.
Ability to configure custom headers for uptime checks.
The target environment runs on Google Cloud VMs, exposing metadata endpoints.
The uptime check feature sends requests from within the Google Cloud environment.
skip_if: The uptime check feature does not allow custom headers.
Requests to `169.254.169.254` are explicitly blocked or filtered by a WAF/firewall.
The response body of the uptime check test is never visible, and response validation is not supported.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Google Cloud Console and locate the Cloud Monitoring section.
Step 2: Identify the 'Uptime checks' feature within Cloud Monitoring.
Step 3: Examine the configuration options for creating a new uptime check, specifically looking for fields to set protocol, hostname, path, and custom headers.
Step 4: Test a simple external URL (e.g., example.com) to confirm basic uptime check functionality and observe response codes/times.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new Uptime Check with the following parameters:
Protocol: HTTP
Path: /computeMetadata/v1/project/project-id
Custom Headers: Metadata-Flavor: Google
Step 2: Click the 'Test' button to send the request to the metadata server.
Step 3: If the test returns a 200 OK status code, the SSRF is confirmed. However, the response body is not directly visible.
Step 4: To retrieve the response body, configure 'Response validation' within the Uptime Check settings. Start by checking for the existence of common characters (e.g., 'a', 'b', 'c', ...).
Step 5: Incrementally build up the expected response string by appending/prepending characters, using the response validation feature to confirm if the partial string exists in the response body.
Step 6: Automate this character-by-character guessing process using a script to extract the full metadata response.
payloads_or_patterns: Response validation patterns will be incrementally built based on initial character findings (e.g., checking for 'a', then 'ca', then 'caa', etc.)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and analyzing requests, though the exploit leverages the console UI directly)
Python script (as provided in the writeup for automated response parsing: https://gist.github.com/nechudav/0b2e0217ffe31a3cd1c1743c590595e6)
success_indicators: Uptime check test returns a 200 OK status code.
Response validation successfully matches specific characters or strings derived from the metadata endpoint.
Automated script successfully parses and returns sensitive metadata, such as project ID, public SSH keys, instance details, or service account tokens.
failure_indicators: Uptime check test returns a non-200 status code (e.g., 400, 403, 500) for the metadata endpoint.
Response validation fails to match any characters, indicating the endpoint is unreachable or responses are not as expected.
WAF blocks requests to the metadata server or requires specific, unguessable headers.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can access sensitive metadata from Google Cloud instances, including project information, service account credentials, and potentially SSH keys, which could lead to further compromise of cloud resources.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing cloud services with uptime checks or similar outbound request features, always consider the possibility of internal network reconnaissance, especially targeting metadata services accessible from within the cloud environment.

meta.title: Firefox for Android: LAN-Based Intent Triggering

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker is on the same local wireless network as a vulnerable version of Firefox for Android.
prerequisites: Attacker is on the same local network as the target.
Target is running Firefox for Android version 68.11.0 or below.
applicability_signals: Target device running an older version of Firefox for Android.
SSDP traffic observed on the local network.
skip_if: Target device is running a patched version of Firefox for Android.
WAF or network security prevents SSDP traffic.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target devices running vulnerable Firefox for Android versions on the local network.
Step 2: Observe network traffic for SSDP announcements.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a malicious SSDP server on the local network.
Step 2: Craft an SSDP announcement that includes an Android Intent URI.
Step 3: Send the malicious SSDP announcement to the target device.
Step 4: The vulnerable Firefox for Android app on the target device will process the Intent URI and launch an activity without user interaction.
payloads_or_patterns: SSDP Announcement containing an Android Intent URI (e.g., `intent://<action>#Intent;scheme=<scheme>;package=<package>;end`)
tools_recommended: Custom SSDP server implementation
Network traffic analysis tools (e.g., Wireshark)
success_indicators: A specific Android activity is launched on the target device without user interaction.
The launched activity performs an action as defined by the attacker's Intent URI (e.g., opening a specific URL, launching another app).
failure_indicators: No Android activity is launched on the target device.
Firefox for Android displays an error or ignores the Intent.
Target device is running a patched version of Firefox.

playbooks.impact: An attacker on the same local network can trigger arbitrary Android Intents on a victim's device running a vulnerable version of Firefox for Android. This can lead to the launch of specific URLs in the browser, opening other applications, or executing other defined actions without user consent or knowledge.
